title: Observability & ops: implementation playbook and execution checklist for AtlasHealth
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>
To: Evan Cole <evan.cole@atlashealth.com>, Sara Liu <sara.liu@atlashealth.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 12 May 2025 09:10:00 -0700
Subject: Observability & ops: implementation playbook and execution checklist for AtlasHealth

Hi Evan / Sara —

Thanks again for the kickoff yesterday. This thread is to share a concise execution playbook for the observability + ops handoff so we can align owners and dates. High-level goal: deliver dashboards, tracing hooks, an error taxonomy, alert routing, and a small runbook bundle so AtlasHealth SREs can operate the Redwood inference integration by the production cutover.

Quick ask: please confirm the proposed milestones below and add any internal owners on your side.

Proposed milestones (target dates):
- Instrumentation sprint (tracing hooks): May 19–23 — engineering (Atlas) + Ben for review
- Dashboard templates & alert thresholds: May 26 — Redwood provides JSON & suggested SLO thresholds
- Runbook drafts & error taxonomy: May 28 — initial runbooks, ownership mapped
- Dry run/fire drill: June 2 — simulated incident and on-call verification

Key deliverables I’ll attach: implementation checklist (spreadsheet), sample Grafana dashboards, and tracing hook snippets. Ben will add notes about minimum span points and recommended sampling. Laura will cover owner assignment and runbook templates.

Things to confirm on your side:
1) who on Atlas is the SRE owner for alerts and PagerDuty service?
2) do you prefer alert routing by team (infra / model / app) or by severity?
3) any org-level naming conventions we need to respect in dashboards/alerts?

Next step: if those milestones look good, I’ll schedule 30-minute working sessions for the instrumentation sprint and dashboards review.

Thanks,
Camila Reyes
Customer Success Manager, Redwood Inference
camila@redwood.ai
+1 415-555-0123

Attachments: obs-implementation-checklist.xlsx, sample-grafana-dashboards.json, trace-hook-snippets.patch

From: Evan Cole <evan.cole@atlashealth.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>, Sara Liu <sara.liu@atlashealth.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 12 May 2025 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Observability & ops: implementation playbook and execution checklist for AtlasHealth

Camila —

Appreciate the clear plan. A couple quick clarifications from our side:
- SRE owner: Sara Liu will be the primary SRE owner for alerts/PagerDuty (she’s cc’d).
- Routing preference: we route by severity initially (P1 -> on-call mobile; P2 -> email + Slack), then add team-based routing for noisy signals after stabilization.
- Naming: prefix everything with \"atlas-prod\" for dashboards and alert names.

Milestones look good; small tweak: can we push the dry run to June 3? We have an already scheduled product cutover the evening of June 1.

I’ll ask our infra lead to join the instrumentation sprint sessions.

Thanks — Evan
Evan Cole
Head of Platform Engineering, AtlasHealth
evan.cole@atlashealth.com

On Mon, May 12, 2025 at 09:10 AM Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Hi Evan / Sara —
> 
> Thanks again for the kickoff yesterday. This thread is to share a concise execution playbook ...

From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>
To: Evan Cole <evan.cole@atlashealth.com>, Sara Liu <sara.liu@atlashealth.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 14 May 2025 08:55:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Observability & ops: implementation playbook and execution checklist for AtlasHealth

Thanks Evan — June 3 for the dry run is fine, I’ve updated the checklist.

I’ve attached a slightly opinionated checklist (obs-implementation-checklist.xlsx) that breaks tasks into: instrumentation, dashboards/alerts, runbooks, and verification. A few notes from our side:
- Dashboards: we’ll provide a minimal \"production health\" dashboard (request throughput, 95th/99th latencies, error rate, cache hit rate) and a higher-detail \"model quality\" dashboard for reranking/generation quality signals.
- Tracing hooks: recommended span points are \"inference.request.start\", \"model.decode\", \"response.postprocess\" and a KV-cache span when applicable. We recommend including a request_id tag and model_version tag on all spans.
- Error taxonomy (short): client_input_error, model_inference_failure, rate_limited, infra_timeout, integration_protocol_error. These map to suggested alert severities in the checklist.
- Alert routing: Redwood suggests P1->PagerDuty (mobile escalation), P2->Slack + Email, P3->email digest. For model_inference_failure, add a 10-minute aggregation window to reduce noise.

Action items for Atlas (please add owners inline in the spreadsheet):
- Confirm PagerDuty service name and escalation policy (Sara)
- Run dependency list for tracing instrumentation (Atlas infra engineer)
- Confirm retention requirements for traces/logs (legal/compliance)

I’ll schedule two 30-minute sessions on May 20 (tracing) and May 27 (dashboards & alerts). Please accept if those work.

Cheers,
Camila

Attachments: obs-implementation-checklist.xlsx

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>, Evan Cole <evan.cole@atlashealth.com>, Sara Liu <sara.liu@atlashealth.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 16 May 2025 10:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Observability & ops: implementation playbook and execution checklist for AtlasHealth

Folks —

Adding a few technical constraints and guidance from the runtime team before the instrumentation sprint:
1) Sampling: for production we recommend adaptive tracing with a 1% baseline and rule-based 100% for requests exceeding 500ms or returning errors. This keeps costs predictable while preserving SLO investigation fidelity.
2) Minimal span set: keep spans shallow to limit overhead — request.start, model.encode (if applicable), model.decode, postprocess, kv_cache_hit/miss. Use tags: request_id, model_name, model_revision, customer_tier.
3) Trace export format: we’ll share a patch (trace-hook-snippets.patch) with lightweight middleware for the most common frameworks (Go/Node/Python). Please slot these into your service wrappers and add the request_id propagation.
4) Alerts: the \"model_inference_failure\" alert should fire on a 5-minute burn rate > 2% of requests or a 10-minute aggregate error ratio > 1.5% depending on traffic profile. We can help tune thresholds after one week of baseline telemetry.

Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.ai

Attachments: trace-hook-snippets.patch

From: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Evan Cole <evan.cole@atlashealth.com>, Sara Liu <sara.liu@atlashealth.com>
Cc: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Sun, 18 May 2025 16:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Observability & ops: implementation playbook and execution checklist for AtlasHealth

Hi team —

Thanks for the confirmations. I mapped owners and next steps in the spreadsheet and want to call out a couple of operational items before we lock dates:

- Runbook ownership: Sara -> Alert triage & PagerDuty; Atlas infra engineer (TBD) -> Instrumentation deployment; Camila/Redwood -> Runbook templates and postmortem checklist.
- Ops access: please confirm whether Atlas wants read-only access to Redwood Console dashboards or a scheduled export. If you choose read-only, we’ll provision atlas-prod-viewer role (audit logs will show access).
- Dry run: June 3 at 09:00 PT — Camila will run the scenario that includes a simulated model_inference_failure and a dependent infra_timeout. Expect a 45-minute session with a 15-minute retrospective.

Sara/SREs: when you confirm the PagerDuty service name I’ll create the P1/P2/P3 bindings and the sample alert rules in our sandbox account for you to validate.

Laura Bennett
Implementation Lead, Redwood Inference
laura_bennett@redwood.ai

title: Finalize payment route (INV-9283) - bank details & VAT
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Emma Walsh <emma.walsh@horizonai.com>, AP Team <ap@horizonai.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 15 May 2025 14:02:00 -0700
Subject: Payment routing question: INV-9283

Hi Emma / AP team,

Following up on invoice INV-9283 (due 05/25). We wanted to confirm your preferred remit route so funds post against the correct legal entity and VAT treatment. Options we can accept: ACH (USD), wire (USD/EUR), or virtual card.

Quick asks so we can finalize:
- Which remit method will you use (ACH / Wire / Card)?
- If wire, please confirm whether you need our EU entity details (for VAT) or the US entity.
- If you choose card, do you need a fee waiver approval?

I’ve attached a proforma and our bank file placeholders so you can see the required remittance reference.

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Revenue Operations | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0193

Attachment: Proforma_INV_9283.pdf (PDF, 48KB)

---
From: Emma Walsh <emma.walsh@horizonai.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: AP Team <ap@horizonai.com>
Date: Thu, 15 May 2025 16:10:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Payment routing question: INV-9283

Ben —

Thanks for the note. We plan to remit by wire but need the following before release:
1) Wire instructions for the beneficiary (bank name, account, IBAN/SWIFT).
2) Confirmation of which legal entity appears on the invoice for VAT purposes (we are booking to HorizonAI EU entity).
3) W‑9 or tax document for our AP file (we can accept 1 file).

Can you either send those details or loop in your finance contact? Also, if wire is the path we’ll include the invoice number in the remittance reference; please confirm the exact string we should use.

—Emma
Emma Walsh
Head of Finance Operations | HorizonAI
emma.walsh@horizonai.com

---
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 16 May 2025 09:08:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Payment routing question: INV-9283 (HorizonAI)

Laura —

Forwarding Horizon’s ask. Can you:
- Confirm the correct remit bank details (wire + ACH + any EU entity alternative)
- Attach current W‑9 and the bank instruction PDF we use for customers
- Quick note on whether we can offer a virtual card fee waiver for this account (HorizonAI) — they asked.

They also want the exact remittance reference we expect so the payment auto-applies.

Thanks,
Ben

---
From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 17 May 2025 11:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Payment routing question: INV-9283 (HorizonAI)

Ben —

Attached: Redwood_Wire_Instructions.pdf and W9_Redwood.pdf.

Wire instructions (USD):
Bank: First Pacific Bank
Account name: Redwood Inference, Inc.
Account number: 3456789012
Routing (ABA): 121000358
SWIFT: FPACUS33

If Horizon prefers to pay EU entity (EUR), send to:
Account name: Redwood Inference Europe B.V.
IBAN: NL91RABO0315273635
BIC: RABONL2U

Remittance reference: PLEASE USE EXACT: INV-9283 / HZN-AI
(Please include both so our cash app maps to the correct customer entity.)

On virtual card: we can accept it but we do not routinely waive card fees. For HorizonAI (strategic), we can escalate a one-time partial surcharge credit; I’ll approve up to 50% of the fee if Ben confirms and we receive a signed payment confirmation.

Laura Bennett
Sr. Manager, Accounting Operations | Redwood Inference
laura.bennett@redwood.com

Attachments: Redwood_Wire_Instructions.pdf (PDF, 32KB); W9_Redwood.pdf (PDF, 120KB)

---
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Emma Walsh <emma.walsh@horizonai.com>
Cc: AP Team <ap@horizonai.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 18 May 2025 10:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Payment routing question: INV-9283

Emma —

Thanks — looping back with what you requested. I’ve attached the W‑9 and our wire instructions. Quick summary of options so you can confirm:

1) Wire (preferred for speed): use Redwood Inference, Inc. (USD details in attached PDF). Remittance reference MUST be exactly: INV-9283 / HZN-AI
2) ACH (USD): same bank, include invoice reference in the payment notes so auto-apply works.
3) Virtual card: we accept cards. We will submit a one-time credit to partially cover the processing fee (up to 50%) once payment is confirmed and you provide the fee line on remittance.

I’ve also attached the Proforma for your AP team to match. If you confirm wire now we’ll mark the invoice as pending receipt so your payment posts cleanly once it clears.

Next steps:
- Please reply with the chosen method and (if wire) expected send date/time.
- If paying EUR to our EU entity, let us know and we’ll confirm IBAN and VAT wording.

Appreciate the quick turnaround — happy to jump on a 15-minute call if that helps.

Best,
Ben Carter
Revenue Operations | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0193

Attachments sent: Proforma_INV_9283.pdf; Redwood_Wire_Instructions.pdf; W9_Redwood.pdf

title: Optimize toolkit pricing / quantization & batching guidance
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Maria Lopez <maria@clearline.ai>
Date: 2025-05-17T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Optimize toolkit pricing / quantization & batching guidance

Hi Maria —

From: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Maria Lopez <maria@clearline.ai>, Dan Price <dan.price@clearline.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: 2025-05-17T16:40:05-07:00
Subject: Re: Optimize toolkit pricing / quantization & batching guidance

Hi all —

Thanks for the clear questions, Maria. Quick answers and recommended approach below. I pulled results from our internal sim (attached) and called out trade-offs.

Quantization support
- Hosted API: Fully supported FP16 and int8 (per-model). No changes needed to your codepath.
- Dedicated (VPC): Supported for both FP16 and int8 with our runtime as long as your instance images include the Redwood runtime container. We provide quantization-aware kernels; int8 uses post-training static calibration.
- Risk note: For reporting models with deterministic outputs, run a 1k-sample eval before rollout. We typically see <0.6% metric drift with int8 on the reporting model family; FP16 is near-lossless.

Batching & caching recommendations
- Avg seq length ~120 tokens: enable prefix/KV caching with a 256-token prefix retention for frequent conversational contexts.
- Batching window: start with a 20ms batching window during steady-state; in reporting spikes move to an adaptive policy: 5ms latency target, increase autoscale thresholds to add workers at +40% incoming QPS.
- KV cache TTL: 15 minutes for session-heavy reports, evict LRU beyond that.

Recommended Optimize packaging (opinionated)
- Standard: Hosted/Pay-as-you-go. Baseline: $0.0024 per token generated, basic caching, no commit discounts.
- Performance: Hosted or Dedicated light commit (3–6 mo). Baseline: $0.0019 per token with intelligent batching defaults and a 5% usage discount after $15k/mo.
- Enterprise Optimize (Recommended for ClearLine): Dedicated reservoir + Optimize add-on. Pricing starts at $0.0013 per token with the following commit schedule:
   • 6-mo reserve -> 8% discount off list rates
   • 12-mo reserve -> 15% discount + 1 week implementation + dedicated onboarding
   • 24-mo reserve -> 22% discount + extended support SLA

Commercial notes / discounts
- We can structure an annual commit for Optimize into the Dedicated contract to lock-in unit economics.
- One-time onboarding fee for Private dedicated installs may apply (typical $25k — waiver negotiable on 12-mo commitments).

Attachments: I attached an internal discount matrix (optimize-discount-matrix.xlsx) and the sim results used to produce the per-token numbers. See also the perf vs. accuracy trade-off table in the spreadsheet.

If ClearLine wants, I can run a short A/B with int8 vs FP16 on a 5k-sample set and produce a delta report in 48 hours.

—Grace O'Connor
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
grace.oconnor@redwood.com
[Attachment: optimize-discount-matrix.xlsx]

title: Staging & agreement gate plan for MedAlign onramp
preview:
From: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@medalign.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurement@medalign.com
Date: 2025-05-05T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: MAP & sequencing for MedAlign pilot

Hi Ben —

Thanks for taking the call earlier. Procurement asked me to share our high-level timelines and map expectations so legal and security can line up their reviews. We need a quick mutual action plan (MAP) that calls out the MSA/DPA/order form gating dependencies and the evaluation window (2 weeks preferred).

High-level constraints from our side:
- Procurement needs a signed order form and commercial terms before provisioning VMs.
- Legal needs to complete redlines on the MSA/DPA before any data ingestion or test traffic.
- Security team requires documentation on retention & audit logs during pilot.

Can you confirm what pace Redwood typically sees for (a) internal legal review, (b) provisioning after order form signature, and (c) an acceptance definition for the 2-week evaluation? Attaching our RFP summary.

Attachments: MedAlign_RFP_summary.pdf

Thanks,
Samantha
Samantha Lee
Sr. Procurement Manager | MedAlign Health


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@medalign.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>
Date: 2025-05-06T10:08:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP & sequencing for MedAlign pilot

Hi Samantha —

Appreciate the summary. Quick answers up front:
- Legal review (MSA/DPA): typical turn ~5-10 business days for first pass depending on complexity; we can prioritize if you need an accelerated slot.
- Provisioning after order form: once signed we can provision dedicated staging in 48–72 hours (config-dependent).
- Evaluation acceptance: we propose a functional checklist + performance SLAs (latency P95 < 300ms for key routes) and a verification run during week 2.

I attached a draft MAP (spreadsheet) with milestone owners and a redline summary for the MSA/DPA so your legal can see sequencing expectations. I also added a suggested gating flow:
1) Order form signed -> 2) MSA/DPA final comments resolved -> 3) Provisioning & creds -> 4) 2-week pilot (canary + full test) -> 5) Acceptance sign-off -> production onboarding.

If that order is a blocker for procurement, we can discuss allowing a limited test account under a PO + temporary NDA until MSA is finalized, but my preference is to keep order form first.

title: Proposal: 12-month prepay + performance kicker with staged true-up
preview:
From: Elaine Morales <elaine.morales@brightlinehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurement@brightlinehealth.com
Date: Sun, 04 May 2025 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Billing cadence — exploring annual prepay option

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the demo last week — the latency/cost numbers look promising. Our finance team asked me to follow up: Brightline is looking to move our Redwood usage from monthly billing to a 12-month prepaid commitment for FY26, but we need an approach that:

- Preserves predictability for our budget team
- Includes a mechanism to protect us if usage ramps lower than expected (we don’t want to overpay)
- Gives us a compelling net price vs. monthly billing

Can you share a proposal with a straightforward prepay amount, suggested ramp/true-up cadence, and any performance or credit line items you can offer if we miss throughput targets or you miss agreed latency SLOs? We’re aiming to finalize commercial terms by mid-May.

—Elaine
Elaine Morales
Head of Platform Finance, Brightline Health


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Elaine Morales <elaine.morales@brightlinehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 05 May 2025 10:34:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Billing cadence — exploring annual prepay option

Hi Elaine — great to hear from you and glad the demo resonated. I put together an initial outline below; attaching a term sheet and a usage baseline export for context. High level:

Proposal highlights (summary):
- 12-month prepay equal to 9 months of current run-rate (net effective ~25% discount vs monthly list)
- Staged true-up: quarterly lookbacks with two-way true-up at 6 months and final true-up at month 12
- Ramp safeguard: if monthly usage falls >30% vs baseline for a quarter, customer receives a service credit equal to unused prepay proportion capped at 10% of annual prepay
- Performance kicker: if Redwood misses agreed 99th percentile latency SLO more than 3x in any quarter, Brightline receives a one-time credit equal to 2% of the prepay for that quarter
- Optional add-on: dedicated routing (regional) available as a paid add-on — can be added without changing prepay economics

Next steps I suggest:
1) Quick internal alignment on concessions (can Marissa and Karthik confirm the credit caps above?)
2) I’ll send a cleaned term sheet + DocuSign if you’re OK with numbers
3) Procurement can propose contract language for the two-way true-up process

Attaching: redwood-prepay-term-sheet-v1.pdf, usage-baseline.xlsx

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 05 May 2025 13:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Billing cadence — exploring annual prepay option

Ben — quick internal thoughts before we finalize numbers to Elaine:

- 9-month prepay / 12-month term is reasonable for a mid-market account with clear ramp potential.
- Credit caps: keep the ramp safeguard at 10% cap (protects Redwood from large down-usage risk). For the performance kicker, reduce to 1.5% per quarter but allow a cumulative cap of 3% across the year. That gives Brightline a meaningful SLA protection while limiting our downside.
- True-up cadence: two-way at 6 months is ok, but add a one-way mid-year safety that favors customer (i.e., if they outperform by >40% we allow an incremental discount credit applicable to next-year pricing) — call it a growth incentive.
- Approve DocuSign flow once Ben confirms language on manual true-up exceptions (force majeure/maintenance windows).

If Ben wants me to join the customer call for pricing sign-off, I’m available Wed afternoon.

—Marissa


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Elaine Morales <elaine.morales@brightlinehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, procurement@brightlinehealth.com
Date: Wed, 07 May 2025 09:22:00 -0700
Subject: Updated: 12-month prepay proposal and protections

Elaine — following internal review we updated the draft to reflect conservative protections on both sides. Key changes from the initial outline: 

- Prepay amount: 9 months of baseline run-rate (same) — net effective discount ~25% vs monthly list
- Ramp safeguard: two-way quarterly lookback. If usage drops >30% in a quarter vs baseline, customer receives a proportional service credit up to 10% of annual prepay.
- Performance credits: 99th percentile latency miss now triggers a credit of 1.5% of that quarter’s notional prepay; cumulative cap 3% for the year.
- Growth incentive: if Brightline outperforms baseline by >40% in any two consecutive quarters, Redwood will apply a one-time credit equal to 2% of annual prepay to be used against next-year commitment or invoice.
- Exceptions: scheduled maintenance windows (with 72h prior notice) and force majeure events are excluded from SLA crediting; manual true-up adjustments can be proposed by either party with documented justification.

I attached an updated term sheet (redwood-prepay-term-sheet-v1.pdf) with marked changes and a clean DocuSign link for signatures. Happy to jump on a call to walk through the mechanics — Wed 3pm PT works for me.

Best,
Ben

Attachments: redwood-prepay-term-sheet-v1.pdf


From: Elaine Morales <elaine.morales@brightlinehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurement@brightlinehealth.com, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 12 May 2025 16:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Updated: 12-month prepay proposal and protections

Ben / Marissa — thank you. This is very helpful and addresses the finance team’s concerns. A couple quick clarifications from procurement:

1) Can the quarterly true-up be automated using usage reports (we can share API access or S3 exports)?
2) For the growth incentive, Brightline prefers the credit applied as an invoice credit (not a prospective discount) — can you confirm that is acceptable?

We’d like to proceed with the DocuSign provided the answers to the two bullets are OK; procurement will table redlines if necessary. If everything checks out we’ll sign by EOD Thursday.

—Elaine


(doc signatures pending: https://docusign.com/transaction/0A1B2C3D)

- Exceptions: scheduled maintenance windows (with 72h prior notice) and force majeure events are excluded from SLA crediting; manual true-up adjustments can be proposed by either party with documented justification.

I attached an updated term sheet (redwood-prepay-term-sheet-v1.pdf) with marked changes and a clean DocuSign link for signatures. Happy to jump on a call to walk through the mechanics — Wed 3pm PT works for me.

Best,
Ben

Attachments: redwood-prepay-term-sheet-v1.pdf

2) For the growth incentive, Brightline prefers the credit applied as an invoice credit (not a prospective discount) — can you confirm that is acceptable?

preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>

Date: 2025-05-08T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Control plane / data plane runbook & wiring diagrams — next steps

Hi Jared / Sarah,

Thanks again for the walkthrough this morning. Per our session I’m attaching the first draft of the private VPC runbook and the initial wiring diagram for your network team to review. The goal here is to capture the control plane / data plane split we discussed plus the operational handoffs for cutover.

Open items we still need from Acme to finalize the runbook:
- Public/Private IP ranges (CIDR) you want assigned to the inference pods and to the control plane management interfaces
- Preferred BGP ASN and whether you will advertise /24s or use more specific prepends
- Egress policy: do you require an egress proxy or direct NAT for telemetry/metrics?
- KMS/HSM location: do you want keys provisioned in your cloud KMS or kept in our customer-managed HSM?
- Acceptance criteria for CANARY (SLA targets, perf test suites, allowed token rates)
- Maintenance windows and change freeze windows for initial cutover

Next steps I propose:
1) You review the attached wiring PDF and mark diagram comments in the Google Drive link (shared below).
2) We have a 45-min joint runbook walk-through next week to assign owners and confirm acceptance criteria. Suggested slots: Tue/Wed morning PT.
3) Update runbook and produce an operations playbook for failover and rollback.

Attachments: [Attachment: redwood-vpc-runbook-draft.md (Markdown)], [Attachment: wiring-diagram-v1.pdf (PDF)]

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com



From: Jared Collins <jared.collins@acmetech.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Sarah Lee <sarah.lee@acmetech.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi@redwood.com>
Date: 2025-05-09T10:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Control plane / data plane runbook & wiring diagrams — next steps

Thanks Ben — reviewed the attachments quickly and a few clarifying questions before we schedule the run-through: 

- VLAN / segmentation: do you expect tagged VLANs across our leaf switches for tenant separation, or will segmentation be done purely via routing policies on the inference nodes?
- Firewall rules: can you provide a minimal egress ACL we can insert at the edge? Our security team prefers explicit allow lists rather than broad NAT.
- BGP: we require graceful restart support and a max-prefix guard. Can Redwood support GR and graceful withdraw on failover?
- Metrics egress: if we go through our proxy (outbound via 10.8.0.0/24), will Redwood be able to push metrics to our Datadog endpoint behind that proxy or do we need to open direct egress to the public Datadog ingest?
- Operational ownership: who will be the primary on-call for the control plane during staging cutover?

I’ve attached our network constraints spreadsheet which includes CIDRs, peering VLANs, and timing blackouts. Once you confirm the VLAN strategy and the minimal ACL set I’ll loop in our infra ops to sign off.

Attachments: [Attachment: acme-network-constraints.xlsx (Excel)]

Jared Collins
Infrastructure Lead, Acme Tech
jared.collins@acmetech.com

On Thu, May 8, 2025 at 09:12 AM Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com> wrote:
> (original message quoted)



From: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
To: Jared Collins <jared.collins@acmetech.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Naomi Feldman <naomi@redwood.com>
Date: 2025-05-10T08:47:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Control plane / data plane runbook & wiring diagrams — next steps

Jared — great questions. I wanted to outline two deployment patterns we typically present so we’re all aligned on tradeoffs; I’ll also upload a short options matrix after this mail.

Option A — Split control plane (recommended for regulated tenants):
- Control plane lives in a management VPC (customer-managed) with peering to the data plane overlay
- Control plane endpoints use private management IP space; data plane nodes communicate via GRPC through an encrypted tunnel (mTLS)
- Operational separation allows you to apply stricter IAM and KMS policies to control plane hosts
- Slightly more complex routing (BGP on the on-prem leaf to management fabric) but clearer audit boundary

Option B — Co-located control + data (faster to deploy):
- Control binaries run in same VPC as inference pods but on dedicated subnets
- Easier routing and fewer peering objects, but harder to enforce strict key separation and tiered access control

Recommendations / answers to your bullets:
- VLANs: we can support tagged VLANs on the leaf and map them to tenant subnets; we often recommend VLAN+subnet for on-prem to simplify NW policy enforcement.
- Firewall ACL: I’ll attach a minimal ACL template. For the runbook we will provide a least-privilege list (control plane management ports, data plane egress for telemetry, NTP, and DNS).
- BGP: Redwood runtime supports graceful restart and will advertise only the prefixes you instruct. We can enable max-prefix limits on our eBGP session.
- Metrics: recommended route is to allow our telemetry collector to talk to your proxy endpoint (10.8.0.0/24). If you prefer direct public egress we can provide a hardened egress list instead.
- On-call: Naomi is the primary on-call from Redwood for the initial staging cutover; Ben will be the technical lead during the run.

I uploaded a short matrix: deployment-options-matrix.csv (see attachments) — this should help your infra ops compare the operational burden. Could we lock a 45-minute slot on Tue 05/13 10:00-10:45 PT for the run-through?

Thanks,
Laura Bennett
Senior Product Manager, Redwood Inference
laura@redwood.com



From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Jared Collins <jared.collins@acmetech.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi@redwood.com>

title: Signal-based lifecycle campaign alignment (avoid duplicate touches w/ AE outreach)
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Tyler Benson <tyler.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Isabelle Laurent <isabelle.laurent@redwoodinference.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, May 10, 2025 at 9:12 AM PT
Subject: Signal-based lifecycle campaign alignment (avoid duplicate touches w/ AE outreach)

Team —

As we turn on the PLG signal routing pilot, we need to avoid the “double tap” where Marketing sends a nurture email the same day an AE is reaching out (or vice versa). We already saw this in smaller tests and it reads spammy.

Proposal for pilot (NA cohort):
1) If a company has a qualified PLG signal routed + a HubSpot sales task created, suppress lifecycle/nurture sends for that company for X days.
2) Keep messaging consistent: Marketing copy should point to the same next step as Sales (Dedicated eval / security review) vs generic “learn more.”
3) Add a lightweight “recent touches” property so AEs can see if we already emailed in the last 72 hours.

Can we align on:
- Suppression window duration (7? 10? 14 days)
- Which programs to suppress (all lifecycle vs just “scale/reliability/compliance” tracks)
- How to handle the inverse case: marketing email already went out, then signal fires.

Tyler/Isabelle — assuming this is mostly HubSpot workflow logic + lists, but want your read before we bake it into the spec.

Camila
—
Camila Reyes
VP Marketing, Redwood Inference


From: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Tyler Benson <tyler.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Isabelle Laurent <isabelle.laurent@redwoodinference.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, May 10, 2025 at 9:46 AM PT
Subject: Re: Signal-based lifecycle campaign alignment (avoid duplicate touches w/ AE outreach)

+1. AEs are already complaining about mixed messaging when a customer gets:
- “Congrats on scaling!” from Marketing
- “Do you want to talk to sales?” from us
within the same 24 hours.

My preference:
- Suppress marketing sends for 10 days once an AE task is created from a PLG signal (company-level).
- But DO NOT suppress product/system comms (billing receipts, incident comms, etc.).

Inverse case: if marketing already sent within last 3 days and then signal fires, we still want the AE task but it should say “marketing touched on <date>” so the AE can reference it instead of starting cold.

Stephanie


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Tyler Benson <tyler.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Isabelle Laurent <isabelle.laurent@redwoodinference.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, May 10, 2025 at 10:21 AM PT
Subject: Re: Signal-based lifecycle campaign alignment (avoid duplicate touches w/ AE outreach)

Agree with 10 days for the pilot. Suggest we do it by “touch type” so we can tune without breaking everything:

Suppression rules (draft):
A) When PLG signal is “qualified” AND HubSpot task created => suppress marketing lifecycle emails tagged:
- plg_scale
- plg_reliability
- plg_security
for 10 days.

B) If marketing lifecycle email (above tags) was sent in last 72 hours => AE task still created, but add note at top:
“Recent marketing touch: <campaign name> on <date>. Consider reply/forward to continue thread.”

C) If AE logs outcome = “Not a fit / asked to stop outreach” => suppress those tags for 30 days.

Open question: do we want suppression at contact-level or company-level? I’d vote company-level to keep it simple + avoid edge cases where multiple contacts get hit.

Ben


From: Tyler Benson <tyler.benson@redwoodinference.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Isabelle Laurent <isabelle.laurent@redwoodinference.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, May 10, 2025 at 11:08 AM PT
Subject: Re: Signal-based lifecycle campaign alignment (avoid duplicate touches w/ AE outreach)

This is doable in HubSpot w/o anything fancy.

Implementation sketch (company-level):
- New company property: plg_sales_motion_suppressed_until (date)
- New company property: last_marketing_plg_touch_at (date) + last_marketing_plg_touch_name (text)
- Workflow 1 (trigger: PLG signal qualified OR task created with “PLG Signal Follow-up” template):
  - set plg_sales_motion_suppressed_until = now + 10 days
  - enroll company into suppression list used by lifecycle campaigns (for the tagged tracks)
- Workflow 2 (trigger: lifecycle email sent with the PLG tags):
  - set last_marketing_plg_touch_* properties

For the “AE task note” part: tasks are created by the router service, but we can expose those properties on the company record so the router can include them in the task description (if Engineering wants). Otherwise we can add a standard snippet at the top of the task template telling reps to check last_marketing_plg_touch_*. 

One nuance: if the same company gets multiple signals, the suppression date will keep extending; that’s probably fine for pilot, but we should cap it (e.g., max 21 days) so marketing doesn’t disappear forever.

Tyler


From: Isabelle Laurent <isabelle.laurent@redwoodinference.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>, Tyler Benson <tyler.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, May 10, 2025 at 12:32 PM PT
Subject: Re: Signal-based lifecycle campaign alignment (avoid duplicate touches w/ AE outreach)

+1 to company-level.

Sales Ops asks / guardrails:
- Don’t suppress if there is an active open opportunity already owned (we want normal deal-stage comms). If we do suppress, it should be limited to “top of funnel PLG tracks” only.
- Add an audit trail: when suppression is set, write “Suppressed due to PLG signal task created” to the company timeline.
- We should align the “tags” list with how Marketing actually labels campaigns. If we rely on tags that aren’t consistently applied, the suppression will miss.

Also recommend the 72-hour “recent touch” rule. Reps will stop complaining if they can see what was sent.

Isabelle


From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Tyler Benson <tyler.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Isabelle Laurent <isabelle.laurent@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, May 10, 2025 at 2:37 PM PT
Subject: Re: Signal-based lifecycle campaign alignment (avoid duplicate touches w/ AE outreach)

Decision for NA pilot:
- Suppress PLG-tagged lifecycle/nurture emails for 10 days at the company-level once an AE task is created from a qualified PLG signal.
- Preserve non-marketing operational emails (billing/system) and any deal-stage communications tied to an active opp.
- Add last marketing touch fields to the company record and make sure it’s visible in the AE view.
- Cap suppression extension at 21 days.

Camila + Tyler: please update the HubSpot workflows/properties spec with the above and circulate for final check on Monday.
Stephanie: please let the pilot AEs know the expectation is to reference the recent marketing touch when present (don’t start from scratch).

We’ll revisit after week 1 pilot data (noise + unsubscribe/complaints).

Jordan
Chief Revenue Officer
Redwood Inference

- New company property: last_marketing_plg_touch_at (date) + last_marketing_plg_touch_name (text)
- Workflow 1 (trigger: PLG signal qualified OR task created with “PLG Signal Follow-up” template):
  - set plg_sales_motion_suppressed_until = now + 10 days
  - enroll company into suppression list used by lifecycle campaigns (for the tagged tracks)
- Workflow 2 (trigger: lifecycle email sent with the PLG tags):
  - set last_marketing_plg_touch_* properties

For the “AE task note” part: tasks are created by the router service, but we can expose those properties on the company record so the router can include them in the task description (if Engineering wants). Otherwise we can add a standard snippet at the top of the task template telling reps to check last_marketing_plg_touch_*. 

One nuance: if the same company gets multiple signals, the suppression date will keep extending; that’s probably fine for pilot, but we should cap it (e.g., max 21 days) so marketing doesn’t disappear forever.

Tyler

- Add an audit trail: when suppression is set, write “Suppressed due to PLG signal task created” to the company timeline.

Also recommend the 72-hour “recent touch” rule. Reps will stop complaining if they can see what was sent.

Isabelle

- Preserve non-marketing operational emails (billing/system) and any deal-stage communications tied to an active opp.
- Add last marketing touch fields to the company record and make sure it’s visible in the AE view.

Camila + Tyler: please update the HubSpot workflows/properties spec with the above and circulate for final check on Monday.

title: Control plane IAM consolidation & provisioning questions
preview:
From: Maya Ortiz <maya.ortiz@finedge.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
Cc: security@finedge.com
Date: 2025-05-04T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Control plane access / SCIM mappings for private deployment

Hi Aisha,

Thanks for taking the call yesterday. We have a few follow-ups after our security review of Redwood Private: specifically around how you recommend we model operator/service accounts, SCIM group -> role mappings, and any minimum SAML assertion attributes you require for the control plane. Our security team wants to avoid manual role provision for >100 operators across multiple GCP projects; SCIM automation is preferred.

Key asks: 
- Can you share a recommended SCIM attribute map (group -> controlplane role) for delegated operators?
- Do you support SCIM patch events vs full sync? Any rate limits?
- For SAML assertions, what attributes are mandatory for provisioning and login (email, name, groups, role claim name?)
- If we want to use a dedicated service account for CI/CD that needs API-only access, do you recommend a specific IAM role pattern?

I've attached our initial proposed matrix (id-provisioning-matrix.xlsx) and a sample SCIM payload we tried (finedge-scim-example.json) — we hit a 409 on the first PATCH call during testing and want to understand expected idempotency rules.

Appreciate any concrete examples we can hand to our infra team.

Thanks,
Maya Ortiz
Security Engineer, FinEdge





From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
To: Maya Ortiz <maya.ortiz@finedge.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Date: 2025-05-04T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Control plane access / SCIM mappings for private deployment

Maya — thanks for the clear asks and the attachments. Looping in Laura (identity), Ben (private deployments), and Stephanie (security) so we can give you a single consolidated answer. High level answers below; will follow up with a short mapping doc and JSON examples by EOD.

Quick answers up front:
- We recommend group->role mapping for operator onboarding; SCIM patch semantics are supported (we process PATCH with upsert semantics). Avoid sending duplicate PATCHes for the same external id in a short window — we do eventual de-dup with 409 responses as a signal.
- Mandatory SAML attributes for our control plane: email (primary), fullName, and groups. If you want policy-level claims, include an 'roles' claim with the claim name 'x-redwood-roles' (string list).
- For CI/CD service accounts we recommend a minimal API-only token approach: create a tenant-local service principal, map it to a 'service-api' role that only has generate-token and model-invoke permissions. Avoid mapping CI/CD accounts to full operator roles.

Laura — can you produce a concise SCIM attribute map (group -> role) and an example PATCH payload. Ben — any constraints from Dedicated/VPC side on service principal IP allowlists or JWT issuer expectations? Stephanie — can you confirm if we should include a short note about token rotation cadence for FinEdge's compliance team?

Thanks,
Aisha Rahman
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference



From: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>, Maya Ortiz <maya.ortiz@finedge.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Date: 2025-05-04T13:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Control plane access / SCIM mappings for private deployment

Maya — attaching the SCIM mapping excerpt below and a compact PATCH example. Notes inline.

--- SCIM mapping guidance ---
Recommended group name -> Redwood control plane role
- redwood-ops-admins -> controlplane.operator.admin
- redwood-ops-read -> controlplane.operator.readonly
- redwood-model-eng -> controlplane.model.editor
- redwood-service-accounts -> controlplane.service-api

Key attributes we consume from SCIM user object:
- externalId (preferred): used as stable id if present
- userName: mapped to login email if no externalId
- displayName / name.familyName + givenName -> profile fields
- groups[].value (group display name) -> role mapping

PATCH example (simplified):
PATCH /scim/v2/Users/46
{
  "Operations": [
    {
      "op": "replace",
      "path": "groups",
      "value": [ { "value": "redwood-ops-admins" } ]
    }
  ]
}

We handle PATCH as an upsert for group membership; if a group referenced in the patch is unknown to the tenant mapping, we log and return 422 with a helpful message. 409s typically happen when you try to CREATE a user with an externalId that already exists — in your example the first call was a POST and the client retried. Our recommendation: use PATCH for membership updates and POST only for initial user creation when the externalId is absent.

Attachment: id-provisioning-matrix.xlsx (contains the full mapping + attribute expectations)

Hope this helps — I can export a canned example in both Okta and Azure AD SCIM flavors if you want.

Laura Bennett
Principal Identity Engineer
Redwood Inference



From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>, Maya Ortiz <maya.ortiz@finedge.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Date: 2025-05-05T10:08:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Control plane access / SCIM mappings for private deployment

Maya — quick operational constraints from the Private (VPC/on-prem) side:
- IP allowlists: For our private control plane, operators typically authenticate via SAML SSO through the customer's IdP. We front the control plane with a small set of egress IPs for webhook callbacks; if you need inbound allowlisting for service principals, we support CIDR blocks per-tenant (minimum /28) or mTLS client certs for stronger assurance.
- JWT issuer: If you plan to mint tokens from your own OIDC provider for CI/CD, the control plane accepts any issuer that is configured in the tenant's IdP settings (we validate the issuer, kid, and exp claims). We recommend a unique audience claim 'redwood-controlplane' to avoid cross-tenant token replay.
- SCIM rate limits: 30 PATCH/second per tenant, burstable to 150 for short windows; long-running full syncs should use our bulk import endpoint rather than iterative PATCHes.

If you want, we can provide a terraform example for the network ACLs and a sample mTLS cert rotation playbook.

Ben Carter
Senior Systems Engineer, Private Deployments
Redwood Inference



From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
To: Maya Ortiz <maya.ortiz@finedge.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Date: 2025-05-06T15:48:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Control plane access / SCIM mappings for private deployment

Maya — consolidating next steps and attachments so your infra & security teams have a single checklist. Please confirm whether you prefer Okta or Azure AD canned examples and whether FinEdge will run a bulk import (recommended for >50 users) or live SCIM sync.

Action items (owner / ETA):
- Laura: provide Okta + Azure AD example PATCH payloads and a small mapping JSON. (ETA: 2025-05-06 EOD)
- Ben: draft a small terraform snippet for tenant CIDR allowlist + mTLS note and provide the minimum /28 CIDR example. (ETA: 2025-05-07)
- Stephanie: include a short note on recommended token rotation cadence and central audit log retention for FinEdge auditors (ETA: 2025-05-07)
- Maya / FinEdge: indicate whether you will run bulk import or keep live SCIM sync (reply to this thread)

Attachments included: id-provisioning-matrix.xlsx, finedge-scim-example.json

If helpful, we can schedule a 30-minute follow-up to walk through a test tenant and run a live PATCH test.

Best,
Aisha

title: Security exhibit onboarding & audit playbook
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood-inference.com>
To: Amy Chen <amy.chen@atlashealth.com>, security@atlashealth.com
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood-inference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood-inference.com>
Date: Sun, 4 May 2025 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Security exhibit onboarding & audit playbook

Hi Amy / Atlas Security team —

Thanks for the quick turn here. Attaching our initial security exhibit draft and a suggested pen-test window template to get the procurement/legal review started. High-level asks from Atlas that we want to confirm in this thread: SOC 2 report availability (last 12 months), preferred pen-test/inspection notice windows, and the cadence you expect for evidence deliveries (weekly snapshots vs. monthly bundles).

Proposed immediate next steps:
- 30-min kickoff (technical + legal) this week to align scope and evidence handoff.
- We’ll produce a redline to the MSA/order form that names the security exhibit appendix and enumerates data-flow boundaries.
- Draft a bounded pen-test protocol that restricts tests to non-production staging unless prior approval is given.

Attachments: redwood-security-exhibit-draft-v1.pdf; pen-test-windows-proposal.xlsx; evidence-upload-specs.md

Can you confirm which SOC 2 artifacts you need (Type II report + bridge letter?) and suggest a couple of kickoff times?

Thanks — happy to own scheduling.

Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood-inference.com | +1 415-555-0182

---
(attachment stubs: redwood-security-exhibit-draft-v1.pdf (11 pages), pen-test-windows-proposal.xlsx (spreadsheet), evidence-upload-specs.md (text))

From: Amy Chen <amy.chen@atlashealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood-inference.com>, security@atlashealth.com
Cc: devops@atlashealth.com
Date: Sun, 4 May 2025 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Security exhibit onboarding & audit playbook

Ben —

Appreciate the docs. Quick reactions from Legal + Security:

- We will need Redwood to provide the latest SOC 2 Type II report and any SOC bridge letters covering the most recent changes. Please share via our secure upload link.
- For the security exhibit: Atlas typically requires an evidence cadence of weekly snapshots for the 30 days prior to a pen test plus a monthly bundle thereafter. If your platform cannot commit to weekly exports, propose an alternative and we’ll discuss.
- Pen test scope: we prefer tests to be performed against a production-equivalent but isolated environment; we cannot accept uncontrolled testing against customer data. Will your team confirm staging IP ranges and data segmentation controls?

Also copying DevOps to route the technical folks. Legal will share the MSA redline expectations separately (we normally ask for a limited inspection right with a 30-day notice and explicit scope).

Available kickoff slots: Tue 5/6 10:00-10:30 PT or Wed 5/7 13:00-13:30 PT.

Thanks,
Amy Chen
Senior Counsel, AtlasHealth
amy.chen@atlashealth.com

---
(secure upload requested; Atlas attached a pointer, not a file)

From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood-inference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood-inference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood-inference.com>
Cc: Amy Chen <amy.chen@atlashealth.com>, security@atlashealth.com, devops@atlashealth.com
Date: Mon, 5 May 2025 11:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Security exhibit onboarding & audit playbook

On Sun, May 4, 2025 at 09:12 AM Ben Carter <ben@redwood-inference.com> wrote:
> Thanks for the quick turn here. Attaching our initial security exhibit draft ...

Amy / Atlas team — 

Thanks for the clarity. Internally I reviewed the draft and propose the following guardrails we should include in the redline before the kickoff: 

1) Inspection window: limit to single annual inspection per order unless additional inspections are mutually agreed and tied to explicit compliance triggers (e.g., material changes, data-scope expansion).
2) Pen test scoping: require tests against a production-equivalent isolated environment; explicitly exclude any testing that attempts to access customer data or persistent backups. Requests for broader scope require a documented change request, approved by Redwood engineering + security.
3) Evidence cadence: offer an automated monthly export (encrypted) by default plus the option for weekly snapshots for the 30-day pre-test window. Provide a secure upload path (we recommend SFTP with scoped keys or an Atlas secure collector).
4) Timeboxed access: any remote inspection/forensic access is for a defined window (days/hours) with named Atlas contacts and an agreed on-call list.
5) Data minimization & redaction: Redwood will redact customer PII from evidence bundles where required and provide an index of redactions.

I attached a short edit-list to the exhibit (see updated file: redwood-security-exhibit-draft-v1.pdf -> we will produce v2 after intake). Also: we should not accept unlimited, unbounded inspection language in the MSA; instead the exhibit will enumerate artifacts and acceptable evidence formats.

Ben/Marcus — can you confirm engineering can support an isolated staging endpoint with ephemeral credentials for the agreed pen-test window? Once we have that confirmation, I’ll prepare a redline to the MSA + a short mutual action plan for the kickoff.

Laura Bennett
Head, Privacy & Compliance | Redwood Inference
laura.bennett@redwood-inference.com

(attachment: edit-list for exhibit, 2 pages)

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood-inference.com>
To: Amy Chen <amy.chen@atlashealth.com>, security@atlashealth.com, devops@atlashealth.com
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood-inference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood-inference.com>
Date: Tue, 6 May 2025 15:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Security exhibit onboarding & audit playbook

Hi Amy / Atlas team —

Thanks — confirming the 5/6 10:00 PT kickoff works for Redwood (Ben will host the call). Quick technical confirmations ahead of the meeting: 

- Engineering can stand up a production-equivalent, isolated staging environment and publish the IP ranges and ephemeral credential process by EOD 5/7.
- We will provide the SOC 2 Type II bundle (redacted where appropriate) via the secure upload link you provided by EOD 5/5.
- Redwood accepts the proposed evidence cadence as a starting point: monthly exports by default, weekly snapshots available during the 30-day pre-test window. We will capture this in the exhibit and the MSA redline.

Attached is a draft v2 of the security exhibit that incorporates Laura’s guardrails and our engineering commitments (redwood-security-exhibit-draft-v2.pdf). Marcus will be on the kickoff to walk through the pen-test scaffolding and evidence upload flow.

Mutual action plan (short):
- By 5/5: Redwood uploads SOC2 bundle to Atlas secure collector.
- By 5/7 EOD: Redwood publishes staging IPs + ephemeral credential flow.
- 5/6 10:00 PT: kickoff call (30 mins) — agenda: final scope, notice windows, MSA redline owners.
- Post-kickoff: Redwood to deliver MSA redline with exhibit incorporated (target 5/13).

Looking forward to the call — please confirm the secure upload receipt once you have the SOC docs.

Marcus Lin
Security & Reliability Engineer | Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood-inference.com

To: Amy Chen <amy.chen@atlashealth.com>, security@atlashealth.com
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood-inference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood-inference.com>
Date: Sun, 4 May 2025 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Security exhibit onboarding & audit playbook

Hi Amy / Atlas Security team —

Thanks for the quick turn here. Attaching our initial security exhibit draft and a suggested pen-test window template to get the procurement/legal review started. High-level asks from Atlas that we want to confirm in this thread: SOC 2 report availability (last 12 months), preferred pen-test/inspection notice windows, and the cadence you expect for evidence deliveries (weekly snapshots vs. monthly bundles).

Proposed immediate next steps:
- 30-min kickoff (technical + legal) this week to align scope and evidence handoff.
- We’ll produce a redline to the MSA/order form that names the security exhibit appendix and enumerates data-flow boundaries.
- Draft a bounded pen-test protocol that restricts tests to non-production staging unless prior approval is given.

Attachments: redwood-security-exhibit-draft-v1.pdf; pen-test-windows-proposal.xlsx; evidence-upload-specs.md

Can you confirm which SOC 2 artifacts you need (Type II report + bridge letter?) and suggest a couple of kickoff times?

Thanks — happy to own scheduling.

Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood-inference.com | +1 415-555-0182

From: Amy Chen <amy.chen@atlashealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood-inference.com>, security@atlashealth.com
Cc: devops@atlashealth.com
Date: Sun, 4 May 2025 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Security exhibit onboarding & audit playbook

Ben —

Appreciate the docs. Quick reactions from Legal + Security:

- We will need Redwood to provide the latest SOC 2 Type II report and any SOC bridge letters covering the most recent changes. Please share via our secure upload link.
- For the security exhibit: Atlas typically requires an evidence cadence of weekly snapshots for the 30 days prior to a pen test plus a monthly bundle thereafter. If your platform cannot commit to weekly exports, propose an alternative and we’ll discuss.
- Pen test scope: we prefer tests to be performed against a production-equivalent but isolated environment; we cannot accept uncontrolled testing against customer data. Will your team confirm staging IP ranges and data segmentation controls?

Also copying DevOps to route the technical folks. Legal will share the MSA redline expectations separately (we normally ask for a limited inspection right with a 30-day notice and explicit scope).

Available kickoff slots: Tue 5/6 10:00-10:30 PT or Wed 5/7 13:00-13:30 PT.

Thanks,
Amy Chen
Senior Counsel, AtlasHealth
amy.chen@atlashealth.com

From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood-inference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood-inference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood-inference.com>
Cc: Amy Chen <amy.chen@atlashealth.com>, security@atlashealth.com, devops@atlashealth.com
Date: Mon, 5 May 2025 11:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Security exhibit onboarding & audit playbook

On Sun, May 4, 2025 at 09:12 AM Ben Carter <ben@redwood-inference.com> wrote:
> Thanks for the quick turn here. Attaching our initial security exhibit draft ...

Amy / Atlas team — 

Thanks for the clarity. Internally I reviewed the draft and propose the following guardrails we should include in the redline before the kickoff: 

1) Inspection window: limit to single annual inspection per order unless additional inspections are mutually agreed and tied to explicit compliance triggers (e.g., material changes, data-scope expansion).
2) Pen test scoping: require tests against a production-equivalent isolated environment; explicitly exclude any testing that attempts to access customer data or persistent backups. Requests for broader scope require a documented change request, approved by Redwood engineering + security.
3) Evidence cadence: offer an automated monthly export (encrypted) by default plus the option for weekly snapshots for the 30-day pre-test window. Provide a secure upload path (we recommend SFTP with scoped keys or an Atlas secure collector).
4) Timeboxed access: any remote inspection/forensic access is for a defined window (days/hours) with named Atlas contacts and an agreed on-call list.
5) Data minimization & redaction: Redwood will redact customer PII from evidence bundles where required and provide an index of redactions.

I attached a short edit-list to the exhibit (see updated file: redwood-security-exhibit-draft-v1.pdf -> we will produce v2 after intake). Also: we should not accept unlimited, unbounded inspection language in the MSA; instead the exhibit will enumerate artifacts and acceptable evidence formats.

Ben/Marcus — can you confirm engineering can support an isolated staging endpoint with ephemeral credentials for the agreed pen-test window? Once we have that confirmation, I’ll prepare a redline to the MSA + a short mutual action plan for the kickoff.

Laura Bennett
Head, Privacy & Compliance | Redwood Inference
laura.bennett@redwood-inference.com

(attachment: edit-list for exhibit, 2 pages)

- Engineering can stand up a production-equivalent, isolated staging environment and publish the IP ranges and ephemeral credential process by EOD 5/7.
- We will provide the SOC 2 Type II bundle (redacted where appropriate) via the secure upload link you provided by EOD 5/5.
- Redwood accepts the proposed evidence cadence as a starting point: monthly exports by default, weekly snapshots available during the 30-day pre-test window. We will capture this in the exhibit and the MSA redline.

Attached is a draft v2 of the security exhibit that incorporates Laura’s guardrails and our engineering commitments (redwood-security-exhibit-draft-v2.pdf). Marcus will be on the kickoff to walk through the pen-test scaffolding and evidence upload flow.

Mutual action plan (short):

Team — sharing the first pass of the finserv target list for the regulated workloads ABM pilot.

Asks (by EOD Monday if possible, but any feedback today helps):
1) Additions/removals: what should we *definitely* include or exclude for v1?
2) Prioritization logic: does the scoring match what Sales is seeing (intent + fit + ability to run a 2–4 week pilot)?
3) Routing: please mark NA vs EMEA coverage and suggested AE owner (Stephanie/Lukas — would love your input).

Notes on filters used:
- Regulated/controlled environments (audit + retention requirements; preference for VPC/private)
- Strong platform/ML or security involvement (CISO/IT Security + Platform Eng)
- Workloads where Dedicated/Private is a clean fit (fraud ops, call center summarization, risk/compliance, advisor tools)

Links:
- Working sheet (comments enabled): https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1FinservTargetAccountsV1
- Exported attachment for offline review

Attachment stubs:

Date: Sat, May 3, 2025 at 10:06 AM
Subject: Re: Finserv target account list v1 — review + NA/EMEA routing

Ben — this is a solid start. A few quick notes from NA coverage:

Adds (NA):
- Northbridge Banking (already warming; security team engaged; strong VPC requirement)
- Orion Payments (payments/fraud ops + support; they care about latency + audit)
- Brightline Insurance (compliance heavy; questionnaire style org)
- Regal Wealth Management (wealth/advisor workflows; strict SSO + audit logging)

Removes / deprioritize:
- Any accounts where the obvious first use case is “marketing copy / generic chatbot” with no security buyer. Those won’t convert to Private/Dedicated.
- A couple of the very small credit unions on the list (unless we have an intro) — they’ll eat cycles with procurement without budget for Dedicated.

Routing suggestion (NA):
- Avery: Orion Payments + 3–4 similar mid-enterprise fintech/payment processors
- Priyom: Brightline + insurance cluster
- Diego: the “fast-moving fintech” cluster (if any are truly security-driven)

On scoring: I’d weight “evidence of security process” higher (mentions of SOC2, SAML/SCIM, retention, audit logs). Those are the ones where our talk track lands.

Happy to do a 20 min working session to finalize routing.

— Steph
Stephanie Nguyen
NA Sales Leader
Redwood Inference

Date: Sat, May 3, 2025 at 11:22 AM
Subject: Re: Finserv target account list v1 — review + NA/EMEA routing

+1 to Steph’s point on weighting “security process present.”

EMEA thoughts:

Adds (EMEA):
- 2–3 mid/large retail banks where we can lean on data residency + private networking requirements (even if we start with Dedicated in-region, they’ll ask about Private).
- One payments processor in DACH or Nordics that’s already investing in fraud tooling (often a clear pilot: rerank + summarize + case notes).

Removes / caution:
- Anything headquartered in EU with strict “no US processors” policies unless we *know* we can meet the topology requirement. It’s a fast ‘no’ otherwise.

Routing (EMEA):
- Hannah should own the strict-compliance accounts (SSO/SAML required, audit export requirements).
- I can take 3–5 exec-touch accounts personally if we think they’re good lighthouse candidates.

One request: can we add a column for “data residency / region requirement (known/unknown)” and “deployment preference (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private)”? It will reduce back-and-forth once outreach starts.

Lukas
Lukas Meyer
EMEA Sales Leader
Redwood Inference

- Recommend we separate “intent signals” into two fields:
  1) Redwood intent (site visits, benchmark download, docs/pricing page hits)
  2) Market intent (job postings, vendor comparisons, partner co-sell signals)

Scoring suggestion (simple + explainable):
- Fit (regulated + deployment constraints) = 0–5
- Security buying signals = 0–5
- Technical readiness (platform team exists, pilot-friendly) = 0–5
- Timing/budget indicators = 0–5

Also: if we’re going to use HubSpot lists, let’s lock the account naming conventions now (or the dashboard will be messy).

If you want, I can create the initial HubSpot list + properties once we agree on the columns.

Isabelle

title: Vertical ABM pilot kickoff (regulated workloads) — asset requests + approvals + deadlines
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Elliot Price <elliot.price@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Wallace <ethan.wallace@redwoodinference.com>, Tyler Benson <tyler.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, May 1, 2025 at 9:12 AM
Subject: Vertical ABM pilot kickoff (regulated workloads) — asset requests + approvals + deadlines

Team — confirming kickoff + getting the asset train moving.

Pilot goal (6–8 weeks): validate repeatable positioning + outbound motion for regulated workloads (starting with finserv + healthcare), and generate qualified pipeline for Dedicated + Private.

Working folder (please use this as the source of truth):
https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1uP3pJmK6nT-regulated-abm-pilot-working

Kickoff (today): 11:00–11:45am PT (calendar invite already sent)
Standing cadence: Tues 10:00am PT (30 min) for weekly ABM standup

Asset asks (with deadlines)
1) Messaging + approved claims (Owner: Soojin; approvers: Aisha/Naomi)

title: Cartwave Hosted API evaluation — scheduling and prework
preview:
From: noreply@redwood.ai
To: hannah@cartwave.ai, ava.chen@redwood.ai
Date: Wed, 30 Apr 2025 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Thanks for signing up — Hosted API evaluation request

Hi Hannah,

Thanks for signing up for a Hosted API evaluation. We've received your request and a member of our team will reach out shortly to confirm a hands-on session (30–45 min) tailored to your PLG use case.

Things you might want to have ready:
- A short list of the API flows you plan to test (generation, embeddings, reranking).
- Sample prompts or datasets (if possible).
- Any compliance constraints (data residency, logging).

If you'd like to skip waiting, you can pick a slot here: https://calendly.com/redwood/45min-hostedapi-eval

— Redwood Inference Team

[This is an automated confirmation message]

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Hannah Cole <hannah@cartwave.ai>
Cc: diego.fernandez@redwood.ai
Date: Wed, 30 Apr 2025 09:20:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Hosted API evaluation request — quick scheduling options

Hi Hannah —

Nice to meet you (virtually) and welcome to Redwood. I'm Ava on the PLG intake team; Diego (cc) is our SDR who helps with environment provisioning.

Would any of the following work for a 30–45 minute hosted API walkthrough and sandbox test?
- Wed 30 Apr, 11:00–11:45 PDT
- Thu 1 May, 09:00–09:45 PDT
- Thu 1 May, 15:00–15:45 PDT

If none of those fit, feel free to pick a slot directly on our calendar: https://calendly.com/redwood/45min-hostedapi-eval

Agenda I'll plan for:
1) Quick intro + goals (5 min)
2) Live Hosted API demo with your sample prompts (20–25 min)
3) Sandbox access + next steps (5–10 min)

Prework: if you can paste 2–3 representative prompts and any constraints (token budgets, latency targets), that will help us prioritize what to demo.

Thanks — I'll hold the 11:00 PDT slot for a short window and will confirm once you reply.

Best,
Ava Chen
SDR Intake — Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0198

From: Hannah Cole <hannah@cartwave.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 30 Apr 2025 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API evaluation request — quick scheduling options

Thanks, Ava —

Appreciate the fast follow. The 11:00 PDT slot should work, but small note: our engineering lead will join from EST and might ask to push to 12:30 PDT / 15:30 EDT if possible.

We care mostly about:
- Low-latency generation for conversational flows (~95th percentile < 300ms)
- Embedding throughput for batch indexing
- Cost per 1k tokens and options to control spend (batching/quantization)

I'll attach a short list of sample prompts in a follow-up (team still polishing).

Hannah Cole
Product Manager — Cartwave
hannah@cartwave.ai

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Hannah Cole <hannah@cartwave.ai>
Cc: diego.fernandez@redwood.ai, grace.oconnor@redwood.ai
Date: Wed, 30 Apr 2025 10:15:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API evaluation request — confirmed time and sandbox access (updated)

Thanks for the heads up, Hannah — 12:30 PDT / 15:30 EDT works for us. Confirmed: Thu 1 May, 12:30–13:15 PDT (45 min).

I've included Grace from Solutions to join the technical portion. Diego will handle provisioning and will drop sandbox credentials once the slot is locked.

I've attached:
- cartwave-demo-prep-checklist.pdf (short prep + suggested prompts)
- redwood-sandbox-credentials.txt (temporary access token + sandbox URL)

Please review the checklist — important callouts:
- Sandbox is rate-limited; if you need a heavier baseline we can request a short lift (ask Diego).
- If you have strict data residency requirements, flag them early so we can route to the appropriate region.

If anything changes, reply to this thread and we'll pivot. Looking forward to it.

Best,
Ava Chen
SDR Intake — Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0198

title: Bluepine Labs — Dedicated fleet throughput & SLO headroom
preview:
From: Erin Wade <erin.wade@bluepinelabs.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.sh>
Cc: Sanjay Rao <sanjay@bluepinelabs.ai>
Date: Tue, 15 Apr 2025 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Bluepine fleet needs — initial throughput profile

Hi Ben —

Thanks again for the demo last week. We want to start sizing a Dedicated pool to support our inference pipeline for the genomics annotation service. High-level:
- Peak inference window: 09:00–18:00 PT weekdays
- Steady-state: ~120k tokens/minute (aggregate across endpoints)
- Micro-bursts: up to 1.2M tokens over 30s during batch ingests (rare, ~3x/day)
- Latency target (p95): <= 250ms for the primary model (13B transformer, decoder-only)

Can you share a rough tokens/sec per-GPU estimate for H100 vs A100 for a quantized 13B and a float16 7B? Also curious about recommended burst headroom and whether Redwood can auto-fallback to a smaller model if we hit capacity.

Attaching a quick usage snapshot so you can see our token distribution.

Thanks,
Erin
Erin Wade
Head of Platform
Bluepine Labs

Attachment: bluepine_usage_snapshot.xlsx (xlsx, 42KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.sh>
To: Erin Wade <erin.wade@bluepinelabs.ai>
Cc: Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.sh>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.sh>
Date: Tue, 15 Apr 2025 11:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Bluepine fleet needs — initial throughput profile

Erin —

Appreciate the snapshot. Quick answers up-front, then proposing a short workshop with Mina and Karthik to lock the numbers down:

Quick estimates (ballpark, single-request generation average, no KV cache warming):
- H100 (fp16, 13B): ~900–1,200 tokens/sec per GPU
- A100 (fp16, 13B): ~500–700 tokens/sec per GPU
- H100 (int8/quant, 13B): ~1,300–1,800 tokens/sec per GPU (depends on quant profile)
- H100 (fp16, 7B): ~2,200–3,000 tokens/sec per GPU

Given your steady-state and burst descriptions, you’ll want committed slabs sized for steady-state + 30–40% headroom and an on-demand burst pool for the 30s spikes. Redwood supports model fallback routing — we can route to a 7B quant variant automatically when a threshold is tripped.

Proposed next step: 30–45m sizing workshop (tomorrow or Thursday). Mina, Karthik — can you prepare a 1-pager with tokens/sec assumptions by GPU SKU and a simple headroom matrix? I’ve attached a rough runbook note for context.

Ben
Ben Carter | Account Executive, Redwood Inference
Attachment: initial_runbook_notes.md (text, 6KB)

From: Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.sh>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.sh>, Erin Wade <erin.wade@bluepinelabs.ai>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.sh>
Date: Wed, 16 Apr 2025 08:34:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Bluepine fleet needs — initial throughput profile

On Tue, Apr 15, 2025 at 11:05 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.sh> wrote:
> Quick estimates (ballpark...)

Hi team — attaching a short calc in-line to make the headroom explicit. Numbers below assume typical tokenization for genomic annotations (short-ish tokens per sequence).

Assumptions:
- Primary model: 13B decoder-only, fp16 baseline latency profile
- Secondary (fallback): 7B quant int8 variant for cheap burst absorption
- Average request length: 256 tokens; average response: 48 tokens; effective 304 tokens/request

Per-GPU throughput (conservative midpoints):
- H100 fp16 13B: 1,000 t/s -> ~3.28 requests/sec (304 tokens/request)
- H100 int8 13B: 1,500 t/s -> ~4.93 req/sec
- A100 fp16 13B: 600 t/s -> ~1.97 req/sec
- H100 fp16 7B: 2,600 t/s -> ~8.55 req/sec

Sizing math for steady-state (120k tokens/min = 2,000 tokens/sec):
- If primary runs H100 fp16: needed GPUs = ceil(2,000 / 1,000) = 2 GPUs (nominal)
- Add 40% headroom -> 2 * 1.4 = 2.8 -> round up -> 3 committed GPUs

Burst handling (1.2M tokens over 30s = 40k tokens/sec peak):
- Fallback to 7B quant pool + burst on-demand H100s recommended.
- If fallback triggers fully to 7B quant: 40,000 / 2,600 ≈ 16 H100 GPUs required.
- Practically: commit 3 GPUs, reserve an autoscale burst pool (spot/dedicated) sized to add ~16 GPUs within 60–90s, plus graceful degradation to 7B quant if autoscale is delayed.

SLO headroom check (p95 <= 250ms):
- With 3 committed H100s for steady-state, p95 will be well under target for normal traffic. During full-burst elevated paths, p95 risk rises unless fallback is used or burst pool spins up. Recommend an on-call autoscale policy and a soft-threshold that shifts 30% of traffic to 7B fallback once token/sec crosses 5k for more stable tail latency.

Notes/risks:
- Cold KV cache increases tail latency for early tokens on long sessions. Warm-up policies and prefix caching reduce this cost.
- Quantization profile testing required — some genomics prompts are sensitive to int8 degradation; recommend an eval set before routing live.

—Mina
Mina Haddad | Inference SRE, Redwood Inference

Attachment: (inline calculations)

From: Sanjay Rao <sanjay@bluepinelabs.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.sh>, Erin Wade <erin.wade@bluepinelabs.ai>
Date: Thu, 17 Apr 2025 14:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Bluepine fleet needs — initial throughput profile

Thanks Mina — helpful breakdown. Quick clarifications from our side:
- Our procurement window is tight: legal wants committed pricing in the next 7 business days.
- Bursts are sensitive: if the autoscale adds GPUs slowly ( > 90s ), we need stronger fallback routing to preserve p95.
- Any ballpark on committed monthly cost for 3 H100s + burst buffer that can hit 16 GPUs occasionally? We just need a placeholder for the procurement form.

Erin, Ben — looping to keep procurement/cost aligned.

—Sanjay
Sanjay Rao | Head of Ops, Bluepine Labs


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.sh>
To: Erin Wade <erin.wade@bluepinelabs.ai>, Sanjay Rao <sanjay@bluepinelabs.ai>
Cc: Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.sh>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.sh>
Date: Mon, 20 Apr 2025 16:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Bluepine fleet needs — initial throughput profile

Sanjay / Erin —

Understood on timelines. High-level cost placeholder and options (numbers are illustrative and will be firmed by our pricing team):
Option A — Committed 3 H100s (monthly reserved) + pay-as-you-go burst:
- Committed fee (3 H100s): ~$85k/month (includes reserved capacity discount and basic support)
- Estimated burst spend (if you hit 16 GPUs for short bursts ~3x/day): ~$4k–$10k/month depending on minutes of spin-up and regional pricing

Option B — Commit 6 H100s (covers more burst locally) + smaller burst pool:
- Committed fee (6 H100s): ~$155k/month
- Burst spend: ~$1k–$3k/month

We can also propose a hybrid: 3 committed + guaranteed 8-node burst buffer (higher committed fee, lower variable). I'll open a DocuSign with Option A draft so legal has a concrete placeholder — link below.

Operational ask: we’ll include an autoscale policy that targets spin-up within 60–90s and an automatic fallback rule to route 30% traffic to the 7B quant model once token/sec crosses configurable thresholds. Karthik / Mina — we’ll add the autoscale SLA and the fallback runbook to the proposal.

Next steps:
- Erin: confirm preferred option for procurement placeholder by Wednesday EOD (22 Apr).
- Ben/Karthik/Mina: finalize proposal and send DocuSign.

Thanks,
Ben
Ben Carter | Account Executive, Redwood Inference
Attachment: proposal_draft.pdf (pdf, 210KB)
Related: https://hubspot.company/contacts/BluepineLabs-5421

- Ben/Karthik/Mina: finalize proposal and send DocuSign.

Ben
