title: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 9:12 AM PT
Subject: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)
Attachments: CacheTelemetrySprint_OnePager_2025-03-03.pdf; CacheMetricsCardinalityBudget_v6.xlsx

Team — requesting go/no-go today so we can start implementation tomorrow.

Proposed sprint: Prefix + KV cache telemetry + initial “increase hit-rate” recommendations MVP (internal report / minimal Console stub).

Scope (2–3 weeks):
1) Runtime instrumentation (prefix cache + KV cache):
   - hit/miss counters + partial-hit semantics (per ADR)
   - miss reason taxonomy (stable set)
   - eviction + memory pressure signals
   - wasted prefill metric (privacy-safe, bucketed)
   - sampling + local rate limiting
2) Joinability/tags from gateway/control plane:
   - model_id + model_version_id, route_id, deployment_mode, region
   - strict allowlists; no per-tenant high-cardinality labels in raw metrics
3) Ingestion + cardinality controls:
   - enforce label policy + drop/aggregate forbidden label combos
   - sampling strategy to keep cost stable
4) Dashboards/alerts + runbook:
   - SRE dashboards for triage and regression detection
   - guardrail SLO proposal (internal; no customer SLA)
5) Recommendations MVP:
   - daily batch job that produces per-route/actionable levers (cache bytes/TTL/policy/routing locality/prompt canonicalization)

Key risks (need explicit sign-off):
- Metric cardinality/cost blowup if labels drift (route/model/version alone can be high)
- Runtime overhead: must be near-zero on p95/p99
- Privacy: no raw prompts; only bucketed lengths + hashed/canonicalized features
- Cross-system join correctness (model version IDs, route IDs)

Acceptance gates (proposal):
- Perf canary: p95 latency regression <0.5% at default sampling; no measurable throughput drop
- Cardinality: within budget in attached sheet; ingestion drops/guards tested in staging
- Data QA: dashboards show sane counts across Hosted + Dedicated canary; missing-tag rate <1% on canary

Owners (draft):
- Runtime instrumentation: Omar (lead), Samir (impl), Noah (review), Sofia (approval)
- Platform tags/join keys: Ethan + Amara
- Ingestion/cardinality: Logan (lead) + Nadia + Caleb
- Dashboards/alerts/runbook: Allison (lead) + Sergio + Aisha; approval: Sean
- Perf/overhead: Marcus (owner)
- Rollout/canary: Connor
- Program mgmt: Talia

If approved, I’d like a single-thread “go” from each of you (Runtime, Platform/Telemetry, Reliability/SRE) + any hard constraints we need to bake into the plan.

Thanks,
Ava
CTO, Redwood Inference


---

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 9:34 AM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

Go from Runtime.

Hard constraints from our side:
- Instrumentation MUST be behind config flags, default on only where we’ve validated overhead (staging/canary first).
- Streaming retries + continuous batching semantics have to be correct (no double counting). We’ve been burned here.
- Miss reason taxonomy needs to be stable; don’t iterate labels every other day or SRE dashboards will churn.

Omar is set as lead; Noah will review the final metric definitions vs the ADR before we flip anything beyond canary.

Mateo
VP Engineering, Runtime


---

From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 10:02 AM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

Go from Platform/Telemetry with two conditions:

1) Cardinality policy is non-negotiable:
   - allowlist labels only; no “free-form route name”, no tenant_id, no request_id
   - route_id/model_version_id must be normalized (single source of truth from control plane)
   - prompt-length/token-count must be bucketed (per standards)

2) Ingestion needs an explicit “drop detection” signal.
   We need to know when guards are dropping series so dashboards don’t show false “0 hits”. Please make that part of ENG-4823/4825 acceptance.

Logan/Nadia will own schema enforcement; Caleb will implement the guardrails.

Priya
VP Engineering, Platform


---

From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 10:29 AM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

Go from Reliability.

A few guardrails:
- Please treat this as an operational change. Rollout needs canary gates + rollback plan (Connor + Sean to sign off before prod enablement).
- I want “telemetry pipeline health” on the same dashboard page as cache hit-rate. When data is missing, we need a clear indicator.
- Any p99 regression >1% at canary should block rollout unless explicitly approved by Runtime + SRE.

If we hit these gates, I’m comfortable proceeding.

Neha Kapoor
VP Infrastructure & Reliability


---

From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 11:11 AM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

+1 go from SRE.

Specific SRE asks (so we don’t page ourselves to death):
- Alerts must be “guardrail” and avoid paging on workload mix shifts. Start with ticket-level alerts and promote to page only after we see stable baselines.
- Add an alert on “hit-rate appears to be zero because metrics are missing” (drop detection + missing-tag).
- Runbook must include:
  - how to validate join keys (route_id/model_version_id)
  - how to distinguish real regression vs telemetry failure
  - rollback steps and who owns each step

Allison will drive dashboards + runbook; I’ll approve alert policy before prod.

Sean
Director, Site Reliability Engineering


---

From: Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 12:03 PM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

Thanks all.

From Product side: +1 to proceed. The minimal requirements for the “recommendations MVP” slice are:
- Output is per route + model_version + region (at minimum) and includes a plain-English “why” with suggested levers.
- Must include caveats when confidence is low (e.g., insufficient sample, high workload volatility).
- We should explicitly avoid any recommendation that implies logging prompt content.

If we can land a first internal report + 1–2 example recommendations validated by CS, that’s a win for this sprint.

Paula
Sr PM, Telemetry Requirements / Rollouts


---

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 2:06 PM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

Approved — thank you.

Decisions captured:
- Proceed with the sprint starting tomorrow.
- Must ship behind flags; staging -> canary -> prod only with gates.
- Cardinality policy enforced via allowlists + ingestion guards; add explicit drop-detection metrics.
- Perf gates: p95 <0.5% regression at default sampling; p99 >1% at canary blocks unless Runtime+SRE approve.
- Alerts start ticket-level; page only after baseline stabilization.
- Runbook + telemetry pipeline health indicator are required deliverables.

Talia: please set up a daily 15-min leadership checkpoint this week only (Mon–Fri) to keep decision latency low.

Connor/Allison/Logan/Omar: please reply-all by EOD with the concrete rollout timeline (staging date, canary start, target prod date) + explicit rollback owner.

Ava
CTO, Redwood Inference

- I want “telemetry pipeline health” on the same dashboard page as cache hit-rate. When data is missing, we need a clear indicator.
- Any p99 regression >1% at canary should block rollout unless explicitly approved by Runtime + SRE.

If we hit these gates, I’m comfortable proceeding.

Neha Kapoor
VP Infrastructure & Reliability


---

From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 11:11 AM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

+1 go from SRE.

Specific SRE asks (so we don’t page ourselves to death):
- Alerts must be “guardrail” and avoid paging on workload mix shifts. Start with ticket-level alerts and promote to page only after we see stable baselines.
- Add an alert on “hit-rate appears to be zero because metrics are missing” (drop detection + missing-tag).
- Runbook must include:
  - how to validate join keys (route_id/model_version_id)
  - how to distinguish real regression vs telemetry failure
  - rollback steps and who owns each step

Allison will drive dashboards + runbook; I’ll approve alert policy before prod.

Sean
Director, Site Reliability Engineering


---

From: Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 12:03 PM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

Thanks all.

From Product side: +1 to proceed. The minimal requirements for the “recommendations MVP” slice are:
- Output is per route + model_version + region (at minimum) and includes a plain-English “why” with suggested levers.
- Must include caveats when confidence is low (e.g., insufficient sample, high workload volatility).
- We should explicitly avoid any recommendation that implies logging prompt content.

If we can land a first internal report + 1–2 example recommendations validated by CS, that’s a win for this sprint.

Paula
Sr PM, Telemetry Requirements / Rollouts

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>

Approved — thank you.

Decisions captured:
- Proceed with the sprint starting tomorrow.
- Must ship behind flags; staging -> canary -> prod only with gates.
- Cardinality policy enforced via allowlists + ingestion guards; add explicit drop-detection metrics.
- Perf gates: p95 <0.5% regression at default sampling; p99 >1% at canary blocks unless Runtime+SRE approve.
- Alerts start ticket-level; page only after baseline stabilization.
- Runbook + telemetry pipeline health indicator are required deliverables.

Talia: please set up a daily 15-min leadership checkpoint this week only (Mon–Fri) to keep decision latency low.

Connor/Allison/Logan/Omar: please reply-all by EOD with the concrete rollout timeline (staging date, canary start, target prod date) + explicit rollback owner.

Ava

title: Public docs: Quantization Profiles (route-level enablement) — review before beta
preview:
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 27, 2025 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Public docs: Quantization Profiles (route-level enablement) — review before beta

Hi all —

We’re in final review on the public docs for the Quantization Profile Library (route-level opt-in) ahead of the beta announcement.

Docs PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-docs/pull/18570
SDK examples:
- Python: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-sdk-python/pull/18574
- TypeScript: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-sdk-typescript/pull/18579

What I need from this group by EOD Fri (2/28):
1) Wording sign-off for “opt-in” + what changes when enabled (and what does NOT).
2) Rollback messaging: route-level disable + global kill-switch (and how we phrase it publicly).
3) Support guidance alignment: what we ask customers to do first if they see output differences or latency changes.

Draft checklist (Google Doc link, for comments): Quantization Profiles - Beta Docs Review Checklist
Attachment stub: Screenshot - Console route settings warning copy.png

Key sections to review (in PR):
- “Enabling a quantization profile on a route”
- “Compatibility and fallbacks”
- “Evaluating quality before rollout”
- “Troubleshooting / rollback”

Goal is to merge Monday morning (3/3) to make the beta email + Console help center consistent.

Thanks,
Alex
Developer Experience
Redwood Inference

---

From: Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 27, 2025 at 10:03 AM PST
Subject: Re: Public docs: Quantization Profiles (route-level enablement) — review before beta

Reviewed the Console-related bits.

Two requests so docs match the UX we’re shipping:
1) Please avoid promising “expected savings” in the docs. Console will show an *estimate* but only for models with benchmark coverage. Suggest phrasing: “You may see cost-per-token improvements; actual results depend on prompt distribution, context length, and hardware.”
2) Compatibility warning language: in Console we show “This profile may be incompatible with your deployment (GPU / max context / model variant). If enabled, Redwood will fall back to baseline precision.”

Also +1 to keeping the toggle as per-route only (no tenant default) in the docs for v1.

Dylan
Console & Tooling

---

From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 27, 2025 at 11:18 AM PST
Subject: Re: Public docs: Quantization Profiles (route-level enablement) — review before beta

+1 overall. From release/ops POV, please make these explicit:

- Default behavior unchanged unless the profile is enabled on a route.
- Rollback path:
  a) disable quantization_profile_ref on the route (immediate)
  b) if widespread issue, platform kill-switch disables profiles globally

On kill-switch wording: I’d keep it high-level (“Redwood may disable profiles globally in emergencies”) and keep the operational details internal.

Also: mention audit/change history exists (who toggled, when) since we know Falconbank asked for it.

Connor
SRE / Release Engineering

---

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 27, 2025 at 12:02 PM PST
Subject: Re: Public docs: Quantization Profiles (route-level enablement) — review before beta

A few leadership-level edits:

1) Please do not say “no quality regression” anywhere. We should say “designed to preserve quality” and explicitly recommend customers run A/B evals on their own prompt sets.

2) “Recommended/latest” alias semantics: we should not imply it auto-upgrades without customer action. If the system supports aliases, we need language that sets expectation that profiles are versioned and customers can pin versions.

3) Add a short section: “When NOT to enable” (regulated workloads without evaluation, prompts sensitive to formatting, ultra-low latency routes until validated).

Otherwise looks on track.

Ava
Executive Sponsor

---

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 27, 2025 at 2:47 PM PST
Subject: Re: Public docs: Quantization Profiles (route-level enablement) — review before beta

Agree with Ava.

One more product nuance: beta is allowlisted + opt-in per route. Please ensure the docs say:
- Beta availability is limited (contact support / your account team)
- We publish profiles per model variant (not “all models”)

And please add a “Known limitations (v1)” callout:
- No automatic per-customer recommendation
- No default-on enablement
- Not all deployments/regions have identical profile coverage yet

Aditya

---

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 28, 2025 at 9:31 AM PST
Subject: Re: Public docs: Quantization Profiles (route-level enablement) — review before beta

Thanks — I pushed a round of updates this morning based on the feedback:

Changes in PR (latest commit):
- Removed “expected savings” language; replaced with “may reduce cost per token” + factors.
- Updated compatibility wording to match Console: “may be incompatible… will fall back to baseline precision.”
- Replaced any “no quality regression” with “designed to preserve quality” + explicit A/B eval recommendation.
- Added “When not to enable” section.
- Added “Beta availability” (allowlist) and “Known limitations (v1)” callout.

Open question: recommended/latest aliases.
Right now I wrote: “If you select an alias (e.g., recommended), Redwood resolves it to a specific version at the time you save the route configuration. You can also pin an explicit version.”

Can someone confirm that’s accurate for GA behavior? If not, I’ll simplify to “versioned profiles; pin supported” and drop alias language.

Alex

---

From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 28, 2025 at 10:06 AM PST
Subject: Re: Public docs: Quantization Profiles (route-level enablement) — review before beta

Alias wording you proposed is the safe version. We should avoid anything that implies silent upgrades.

If you want even tighter language:
“Aliases resolve to a specific version when you apply the configuration. Redwood will not change the version on an existing route unless you update the route settings.”

That matches the operational intent and reduces future confusion.

Connor

---

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Mar 1, 2025 at 4:38 PM PST
Subject: Re: Public docs: Quantization Profiles (route-level enablement) — review before beta

Approved with Connor’s suggested alias sentence.

Please also add one line under troubleshooting that points to support with the two common cases we’ve seen:
- “Output differs after enabling” (SUP-27411)
- “Latency regression on dedicated” (SUP-27436)

We don’t need ticket IDs in public docs, but the symptom phrasing should match what Support will see.

Once that’s in, you have my sign-off to merge.

Ava

title: Re: Quantization Profiles: risk review + kill-switch proposal for beta
preview:
From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 21, 2025 at 8:17 AM PT
Subject: Quantization Profiles: risk review + kill-switch proposal for beta

Ava,

Ahead of next week’s beta enablement with the design partners, we did a quick risk review on the Quantization Profile Library rollout path.

Top risks we want to explicitly guard:
1) Quality regressions (offline evals pass but edge-case prompts regress).
2) Incompatibility (profile enabled on a route but traffic lands on unsupported GPU SKU / context length / model variant).
3) Control plane or registry load issues causing elevated 5xx (thundering herd on profile metadata fetch).

Proposal (what we need leadership approval on):
- Add an emergency global kill-switch that forces baseline behavior (ignore any route-level profile refs) across hosted + dedicated + private control planes.
- Make the kill-switch audited + RBAC-restricted (SRE oncall + release engineering), with documented runbook.
- Gate beta rollout with explicit guardrails:
  - compatibility checks in policy engine (deterministic) w/ safe fallback to baseline
  - per-route enablement only (no tenant defaults)
  - canary: 1% → 10% → 50% traffic steps, with rollback criteria
  - dashboards: enabled rate, fallback rate, error rate, p95 latency, cost/token deltas

If approved today, we can keep the release train timing.

Attachments:
- QuantProfiles_RiskReview_v3.pdf
- QuantProfiles_RolloutGuardrails_Checklist_v1.docx

Connor
Connor O’Brien
Release / Rollout Lead, Redwood Inference


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 21, 2025 at 8:34 AM PT
Subject: Re: Quantization Profiles: risk review + kill-switch proposal for beta

Thanks.

Before I sign off:
- What are the exact semantics of the kill-switch? (Does it override everything, including “recommended/latest” aliases?)
- Who can flip it, and how fast does it propagate?
- What’s the blast radius on private deployments (VPC/on-prem)? We need to be clear about what we can/can’t enforce.

Also: do we have a firm stance that incompatibility must *never* error a request (only fallback), unless the customer explicitly asks for hard-fail?

Ava


From: Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 21, 2025 at 8:57 AM PT
Subject: Re: Quantization Profiles: risk review + kill-switch proposal for beta

Jumping in on semantics + controls from Console/tooling side.

Kill-switch semantics (what we’re implementing in Console/backend):
- When kill-switch is ON, resolved profile outcome is always “disabled_by_killswitch” and runtime uses baseline precision. Route config remains intact (we don’t mutate customers’ saved settings).
- We still record the route’s configured ref in audit logs, but we annotate resolution outcome so Support/SRE can explain why it didn’t apply.

RBAC/audit:
- Console will not expose the kill-switch to customers.
- Internal control should be restricted to SRE oncall and release engineering only.
- Every state change emits an audit event (actor, reason, timestamp, previous→new) and lands in our audit export.

Propagation:
- Once flipped, we rely on config distribution (same path as feature flags). Connor can confirm expected propagation window, but we should assume a few minutes worst case.

On incompatibility:
- My strong preference is default to fallback-not-fail in v1 (less customer-visible breakage). If we want hard-fail, it should be an explicit route policy, not default behavior.

Dylan
Dylan Brooks
Console & Tooling Sponsor, Redwood Inference


From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 21, 2025 at 9:18 AM PT
Subject: Re: Quantization Profiles: risk review + kill-switch proposal for beta

Answering the outstanding items.

Kill-switch semantics:
- Yes, it overrides everything (including any aliases). The resolver returns “baseline” regardless of configured profile ref.
- This is intentionally conservative. If we’re in a bad state (quality regression / incompatibility bug / registry incident), we want one lever.

Who can flip it:
- Proposed: SRE oncall + Release Eng (Connor’s rotation). No other roles.
- We’ll require a reason string in the change request (for audit trail + incident tying).

Propagation:
- Hosted + Dedicated: propagated via control plane config cache; typical < 60s, worst-case 5 min.
- Private (VPC / on-prem): depends on customer’s update cadence. We can ship the capability, but we cannot force-enable kill-switch state unless they are connected to our control plane. We should message “kill-switch available” rather than “kill-switch enforced.”

Incompatibility stance:
- Agree with Dylan: v1 default is fallback-not-fail.
- We’ll still surface a warning / error on *enable* (Console/API) if incompatible is detected for the route’s declared deployment target.
- At request time: if a request lands on unsupported hardware/context, policy engine will resolve “fallback_to_baseline” and increment fallback_rate.

Why we need this: we’ve already seen early signals that two failure modes look the same to customers (“model got worse” vs “system is down”). Kill-switch helps separate safety from availability.

Connor


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 21, 2025 at 10:02 AM PT
Subject: Re: Quantization Profiles: risk review + kill-switch proposal for beta

+1 to proceeding with the kill-switch and the guardrails.

Conditions I want baked into the beta launch gates:
- No default-on anywhere. Explicit route-level opt-in only.
- Deterministic resolution: same inputs (route config + model id + hardware class + request constraints) → same result, with outcome logged.
- Canary criteria written down in the checklist and in the oncall runbook (with specific thresholds for fallback_rate and 5xx).
- Confirm we’re protected against registry load spikes (cache + rate limiting). We can’t repeat last month’s “metadata fetch retry storm.”

Ava — if you’re aligned, I’m comfortable approving this plan today.

Aditya
Aditya Rao
VP Engineering, Redwood Inference


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 21, 2025 at 11:36 AM PT
Subject: Re: Quantization Profiles: risk review + kill-switch proposal for beta

Approved to proceed with:
- global kill-switch (override-all, audit + RBAC restricted as described)
- fallback-not-fail as the default runtime behavior in v1
- staged rollout guardrails (canary steps + explicit rollback criteria)

Please ensure the public/customer messaging is consistent: “opt-in per route,” “safe fallback to baseline,” and “clear rollback path.”

Connor: update the rollout checklist with the propagation caveat for Private deployments (we offer the mechanism, but enforcement depends on their control plane connection/update).

text:
From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 21, 2025 at 8:17 AM PT
Subject: Quantization Profiles: risk review + kill-switch proposal for beta

Ava,

Ahead of next week’s beta enablement with the design partners, we did a quick risk review on the Quantization Profile Library rollout path.

Top risks we want to explicitly guard:
1) Quality regressions (offline evals pass but edge-case prompts regress).
2) Incompatibility (profile enabled on a route but traffic lands on unsupported GPU SKU / context length / model variant).
3) Control plane or registry load issues causing elevated 5xx (thundering herd on profile metadata fetch).

Proposal (what we need leadership approval on):
- Add an emergency global kill-switch that forces baseline behavior (ignore any route-level profile refs) across hosted + dedicated + private control planes.
- Make the kill-switch audited + RBAC-restricted (SRE oncall + release engineering), with documented runbook.
- Gate beta rollout with explicit guardrails:
  - compatibility checks in policy engine (deterministic) w/ safe fallback to baseline
  - per-route enablement only (no tenant defaults)
  - canary: 1% → 10% → 50% traffic steps, with rollback criteria
  - dashboards: enabled rate, fallback rate, error rate, p95 latency, cost/token deltas

If approved today, we can keep the release train timing.

Attachments:
- QuantProfiles_RiskReview_v3.pdf
- QuantProfiles_RolloutGuardrails_Checklist_v1.docx

Connor
Connor O’Brien
Release / Rollout Lead, Redwood Inference


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 21, 2025 at 8:34 AM PT
Subject: Re: Quantization Profiles: risk review + kill-switch proposal for beta

Thanks.

Before I sign off:
- What are the exact semantics of the kill-switch? (Does it override everything, including “recommended/latest” aliases?)
- Who can flip it, and how fast does it propagate?
- What’s the blast radius on private deployments (VPC/on-prem)? We need to be clear about what we can/can’t enforce.

Also: do we have a firm stance that incompatibility must *never* error a request (only fallback), unless the customer explicitly asks for hard-fail?

Ava


From: Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 21, 2025 at 8:57 AM PT
Subject: Re: Quantization Profiles: risk review + kill-switch proposal for beta

Jumping in on semantics + controls from Console/tooling side.

Kill-switch semantics (what we’re implementing in Console/backend):
- When kill-switch is ON, resolved profile outcome is always “disabled_by_killswitch” and runtime uses baseline precision. Route config remains intact (we don’t mutate customers’ saved settings).
- We still record the route’s configured ref in audit logs, but we annotate resolution outcome so Support/SRE can explain why it didn’t apply.

RBAC/audit:
- Console will not expose the kill-switch to customers.
- Internal control should be restricted to SRE oncall and release engineering only.
- Every state change emits an audit event (actor, reason, timestamp, previous→new) and lands in our audit export.

Propagation:
- Once flipped, we rely on config distribution (same path as feature flags). Connor can confirm expected propagation window, but we should assume a few minutes worst case.

On incompatibility:
- My strong preference is default to fallback-not-fail in v1 (less customer-visible breakage). If we want hard-fail, it should be an explicit route policy, not default behavior.

Dylan
Dylan Brooks
Console & Tooling Sponsor, Redwood Inference


From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 21, 2025 at 9:18 AM PT
Subject: Re: Quantization Profiles: risk review + kill-switch proposal for beta

Answering the outstanding items.

Kill-switch semantics:
- Yes, it overrides everything (including any aliases). The resolver returns “baseline” regardless of configured profile ref.
- This is intentionally conservative. If we’re in a bad state (quality regression / incompatibility bug / registry incident), we want one lever.

Who can flip it:
- Proposed: SRE oncall + Release Eng (Connor’s rotation). No other roles.
- We’ll require a reason string in the change request (for audit trail + incident tying).

Propagation:
- Hosted + Dedicated: propagated via control plane config cache; typical < 60s, worst-case 5 min.
- Private (VPC / on-prem): depends on customer’s update cadence. We can ship the capability, but we cannot force-enable kill-switch state unless they are connected to our control plane. We should message “kill-switch available” rather than “kill-switch enforced.”

Incompatibility stance:
- Agree with Dylan: v1 default is fallback-not-fail.
- We’ll still surface a warning / error on *enable* (Console/API) if incompatible is detected for the route’s declared deployment target.
- At request time: if a request lands on unsupported hardware/context, policy engine will resolve “fallback_to_baseline” and increment fallback_rate.

Why we need this: we’ve already seen early signals that two failure modes look the same to customers (“model got worse” vs “system is down”). Kill-switch helps separate safety from availability.

Connor

- Confirm we’re protected against registry load spikes (cache + rate limiting). We can’t repeat last month’s “metadata fetch retry storm.”

Ava — if you’re aligned, I’m comfortable approving this plan today.

title: Re: Redwood Inference security review: retention & deletion timelines
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Samir Desai <samir.desai@northpeakbank.com>
Cc: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Feb 18, 2025 at 4:12 PM
Subject: Re: Redwood Inference security review: retention & deletion timelines

Samir  answering inline below.

1) Prompts/outputs retention: For Hosted API and Dedicated, we do not persistently store prompts and model outputs by default.
2) Request logs retention: We retain operational request logs (metadata only) for 14 days by default.
3) Debug payload logging: If you explicitly enable it for troubleshooting, retention is 7 days.
4) Audit logs: retained for 12 months.
5) Deletion requests: once validated, we complete deletion within 30 days for data stored in Redwood-managed systems.

Happy to jump on a quick call if helpful.

 Ava

--
Ava Chen
Enterprise Account Executive
Redwood Inference

title: Re: Northstar Bank  confirmation on inference log retention
Approval: Incident Bot v2 permissions + audit logging (config diff + privileged telemetry)
preview:
From: Kaitlyn Nguyen <kaitlyn.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Feb 12, 2025 at 9:14 AM PST
Subject: Approval: Incident Bot v2 permissions + audit logging (config diff + privileged telemetry)

Hi all — requesting final security/compliance approval on the permissions + audit approach for Incident Bot v2 before we merge the remaining PRs and enable beta for SRE rotation.

Summary of proposed model (details in attached PDF + scope matrix):

1) Service identity + scopes (least privilege)
- Bot runs as service identity: svc-incident-bot
- Two new internal scopes:
  - incident_bot:config_diff:read (customer-safe, redacted output only)
  - incident_bot:telemetry:read_privileged (bounded query templates; tenant-aware guardrails)
- No write scopes. No direct access to raw secret stores.

2) Config diff endpoint access
- Endpoint returns ONLY allowlisted fields by default; everything else is either omitted or redacted.
- Redaction layer runs server-side (not in Slack bot) to prevent leakage if Slack rendering changes.
- For Sev0/Sev1 only: break-glass path allows on-call to request an expanded diff view (still redacted for secrets, but includes more non-secret fields).
  - This requires incident_id + reason, plus elevated on-call role.

3) Privileged telemetry access
- Query service exposes template-based queries (no arbitrary PromQL/SQL).
- Hard limits:
  - time window max 30m (defaults to 15m)
  - max series/rows per query (bounded)
  - per-incident caching + coalescing
  - rate limits + circuit breaker during upstream slowness

4) Audit logging
- Audit event emitted for:
  - every config diff request (including tenant/service identifiers + incident_id)
  - every privileged telemetry request (including template id + bounds)
  - every break-glass elevation attempt (approved/denied)
- Audit events go to SIEM pipeline with retention aligned to current internal standard.

Open items we want sign-off on today:
- Is the break-glass flow acceptable as described (Sev0/Sev1 only, logged, time-limited)?
- Are the audit event fields sufficient for later review/investigation?

Attachments:

- incident-bot-scope-matrix-v2.xlsx

If approved, Ava will update ADR-013 with final language and we’ll proceed with merges for PR-29133 / PR-29140.

text:
From: Kaitlyn Nguyen <kaitlyn.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Feb 12, 2025 at 9:14 AM PST
Subject: Approval: Incident Bot v2 permissions + audit logging (config diff + privileged telemetry)

Hi all — requesting final security/compliance approval on the permissions + audit approach for Incident Bot v2 before we merge the remaining PRs and enable beta for SRE rotation.

Summary of proposed model (details in attached PDF + scope matrix):

1) Service identity + scopes (least privilege)
- Bot runs as service identity: svc-incident-bot
- Two new internal scopes:
  - incident_bot:config_diff:read (customer-safe, redacted output only)
  - incident_bot:telemetry:read_privileged (bounded query templates; tenant-aware guardrails)
- No write scopes. No direct access to raw secret stores.

2) Config diff endpoint access
- Endpoint returns ONLY allowlisted fields by default; everything else is either omitted or redacted.
- Redaction layer runs server-side (not in Slack bot) to prevent leakage if Slack rendering changes.
- For Sev0/Sev1 only: break-glass path allows on-call to request an expanded diff view (still redacted for secrets, but includes more non-secret fields).
  - This requires incident_id + reason, plus elevated on-call role.

3) Privileged telemetry access
- Query service exposes template-based queries (no arbitrary PromQL/SQL).
- Hard limits:
  - time window max 30m (defaults to 15m)
  - max series/rows per query (bounded)
  - per-incident caching + coalescing
  - rate limits + circuit breaker during upstream slowness

4) Audit logging
- Audit event emitted for:
  - every config diff request (including tenant/service identifiers + incident_id)
  - every privileged telemetry request (including template id + bounds)
  - every break-glass elevation attempt (approved/denied)
- Audit events go to SIEM pipeline with retention aligned to current internal standard.

Open items we want sign-off on today:
- Is the break-glass flow acceptable as described (Sev0/Sev1 only, logged, time-limited)?
- Are the audit event fields sufficient for later review/investigation?

Attachments:
- incident-bot-v2-permissions-audit-approval-summary-v3.pdf
- incident-bot-scope-matrix-v2.xlsx

If approved, Ava will update ADR-013 with final language and we’ll proceed with merges for PR-29133 / PR-29140.

Thanks,
Kaitlyn
Authn/Authz & Tenant Isolation
Redwood Inference

title: Re: Northstar Bank — requested LTS commitment language (support window + cadence)
preview:
From: Priya Nair <priya.nair@northstarbank.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Tom Becker <tom.becker@northstarbank.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 10, 2025 at 8:14 AM PST
Subject: Redwood Private + Dedicated — LTS commitment language

Hi Avery,

As part of our change control and vendor risk review, Procurement is asking for concise written language we can include in the security/ops appendix that describes:

1) What “LTS” means for the runtime (support window)
2) Patch cadence (expected frequency + out-of-band security)
3) What’s excluded / not promised (feature parity, etc.)
4) Any commitments for vulnerability response

Can you send suggested language we can circulate internally?

Thanks,
Priya

—
Priya Nair
Director, Platform Engineering
Northstar Bank



From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 10, 2025 at 9:02 AM PST
Subject: Fwd: Northstar Bank — requested LTS commitment language (support window + cadence)

Forwarding below. They want something we can paste into an appendix.

Can you sanity check/wordsmith the below so I don’t accidentally promise an SLA we can’t meet?

Draft language (v0):

“Redwood Runtime LTS Channel

Redwood provides a Long Term Support (LTS) release channel for the Redwood Serving Runtime for enterprise customers using Dedicated or Private deployments. An LTS line is a stability-focused branch intended to remain compatible over an extended period, with controlled, low-risk backports.

Support window: Redwood will support each LTS major.minor line for a minimum of twelve (12) months from initial LTS GA, with patch releases published on a regular cadence.

Patch cadence: Redwood targets monthly patch releases for the active LTS line(s). Security fixes may be released out-of-band when warranted.

Backports: LTS patches prioritize security fixes and critical correctness/reliability issues. New features from mainline are not guaranteed to be included.

Compatibility: LTS patches are intended to be upgrade-compatible within the same LTS line. Redwood will provide upgrade notes and known-issues guidance for each patch.

Artifacts: Redwood will publish verifiable runtime artifacts (checksums and provenance metadata) for Private deployments.”

Questions:
- OK to say “minimum 12 months”? (some folks talked about 6–12+)
- Should we say “targets monthly” vs “monthly”
- Any standard wording for vuln response timeframe? (they asked explicitly)

If we can get a “blessed” paragraph today, that helps keep deal moving.

Avery

—
Avery Johnson
Enterprise Account Executive
Redwood Inference



From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 10, 2025 at 10:11 AM PST
Subject: Re: Fwd: Northstar Bank — requested LTS commitment language (support window + cadence)

This is close. I’d tweak to reduce “contract-y” risk and align with what we’ve been using internally:

- Support window: say “12 months for GA LTS lines” but avoid “minimum” unless Legal already OK’d it. Suggest: “Redwood intends to support…” or “Redwood provides…”
- Patch cadence: definitely “targets monthly” + “may adjust based on risk / customer impact”
- Vuln response: we shouldn’t promise a specific hours-based SLA in an email. Safer: “expedited out-of-band security releases for critical vulnerabilities” and point to our security advisories process.
- Artifacts: good to mention checksums/provenance; avoid saying “SBOM” unless we’re ready to provide consistently.

Proposed v1 (feel free to paste):

“Runtime LTS Channel

Redwood offers a Long Term Support (LTS) release channel for the Redwood Serving Runtime for Dedicated and Private deployments. LTS releases prioritize stability and predictable change, with fixes backported in a controlled manner.

Support window: For each GA LTS line, Redwood provides ongoing maintenance for approximately twelve (12) months from the GA date.

Patch cadence: Redwood targets a monthly patch cadence for the active LTS line(s), with additional out-of-band patches for critical security or reliability issues as needed.

Scope: LTS patches focus on security fixes and high-severity correctness, crash, and reliability fixes. Feature parity with the mainline channel is not guaranteed.

Compatibility & guidance: Patches within an LTS line are intended to be upgrade-compatible. Redwood provides release notes, known issues, and upgrade/rollback guidance with each patch.

Artifact integrity (Private): Redwood publishes cryptographic checksums and provenance metadata for runtime artifacts used in Private deployments.”

If they push on vulnerability timelines, we can route through Security (Ava) for approved language.

Connor



From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 10, 2025 at 11:03 AM PST
Subject: Re: Northstar Bank — requested LTS commitment language (support window + cadence)

+1 to Connor’s edits.

One small addition from Support perspective: we should avoid phrasing that implies every Sev2 gets backported automatically. Customers sometimes read that as a guarantee.

Maybe adjust “high-severity correctness” to “critical correctness/reliability” and add “subject to backport review” (but keep it short).

Also: if they ask “mandatory vs optional patches,” we can say Redwood may recommend a patch when it addresses a critical security/reliability issue.

Dev

—
Dev Patel
Head of Support
Redwood Inference



From: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 10, 2025 at 12:22 PM PST
Subject: Re: Northstar Bank — requested LTS commitment language (support window + cadence)

Agree with the direction.

Two customer-expectation gotchas we’ve seen:

1) “Approximately 12 months” is good; if they need exact dates we can provide a per-line EOL date once that line is GA.
2) Make sure “Dedicated and Private” doesn’t imply Hosted customers can’t use it; better to frame as “primarily intended for Dedicated/Private” if they’re sensitive.

Otherwise the v1 reads well and is something I’m comfortable having in writing.

Rachel

—
Rachel Kim
Enterprise Customer Success Lead
Redwood Inference



From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 10, 2025 at 1:48 PM PST
Subject: Re: Northstar Bank — requested LTS commitment language (support window + cadence)

If they insist on vulnerability response language, use this approved line (keeps us out of SLA territory):

“Security: Redwood monitors vulnerability disclosures and will provide security updates for supported LTS lines, including out-of-band releases for critical issues when appropriate. Redwood will communicate remediation guidance via security advisories and release notes.”

Do not commit to a specific hour/day turnaround in email. If Procurement requires it, we should route through the MSA/SOW with Security + Legal review.

Ava



From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
To: Priya Nair <priya.nair@northstarbank.com>
Cc: Tom Becker <tom.becker@northstarbank.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 10, 2025 at 4:37 PM PST
Subject: Re: Redwood Private + Dedicated — LTS commitment language

Priya — below is suggested language you can include in the appendix. This is written to describe our standard LTS program without creating an hours-based SLA in an email (we can address any required SLAs separately in the contract if needed).

—
Runtime LTS Channel

Redwood offers a Long Term Support (LTS) release channel for the Redwood Serving Runtime, intended for enterprise customers who want a stability-focused baseline for Dedicated and Private deployments. LTS releases prioritize predictable change, with fixes backported in a controlled manner.

Support window: For each GA LTS line, Redwood provides ongoing maintenance for approximately twelve (12) months from the GA date.

Patch cadence: Redwood targets a monthly patch cadence for the active LTS line(s), with additional out-of-band patches for critical security or reliability issues as needed.

Scope: LTS patches focus on security fixes and critical correctness, crash, and reliability fixes, subject to backport review. Feature parity with the mainline channel is not guaranteed.

Compatibility & guidance: Patches within an LTS line are intended to be upgrade-compatible. Redwood provides release notes, known issues, and upgrade/rollback guidance with each patch.

Artifact integrity (Private): Redwood publishes cryptographic checksums and provenance metadata for runtime artifacts used in Private deployments.

Security: Redwood monitors vulnerability disclosures and will provide security updates for supported LTS lines, including out-of-band releases for critical issues when appropriate. Redwood will communicate remediation guidance via security advisories and release notes.
—

If you want, I can also send a short “FAQ-style” version that’s easier to circulate internally.

Best,
Avery

—
Avery Johnson
Enterprise Account Executive
Redwood Inference

Subject: Re: Northstar Bank — requested LTS commitment language (support window + cadence)

If they insist on vulnerability response language, use this approved line (keeps us out of SLA territory):

“Security: Redwood monitors vulnerability disclosures and will provide security updates for supported LTS lines, including out-of-band releases for critical issues when appropriate. Redwood will communicate remediation guidance via security advisories and release notes.”

Do not commit to a specific hour/day turnaround in email. If Procurement requires it, we should route through the MSA/SOW with Security + Legal review.

Ava



From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
To: Priya Nair <priya.nair@northstarbank.com>
Cc: Tom Becker <tom.becker@northstarbank.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 10, 2025 at 4:37 PM PST
Subject: Re: Redwood Private + Dedicated — LTS commitment language

Priya — below is suggested language you can include in the appendix. This is written to describe our standard LTS program without creating an hours-based SLA in an email (we can address any required SLAs separately in the contract if needed).

—
Runtime LTS Channel

Redwood offers a Long Term Support (LTS) release channel for the Redwood Serving Runtime, intended for enterprise customers who want a stability-focused baseline for Dedicated and Private deployments. LTS releases prioritize predictable change, with fixes backported in a controlled manner.

Support window: For each GA LTS line, Redwood provides ongoing maintenance for approximately twelve (12) months from the GA date.

Patch cadence: Redwood targets a monthly patch cadence for the active LTS line(s), with additional out-of-band patches for critical security or reliability issues as needed.

Scope: LTS patches focus on security fixes and critical correctness, crash, and reliability fixes, subject to backport review. Feature parity with the mainline channel is not guaranteed.

Compatibility & guidance: Patches within an LTS line are intended to be upgrade-compatible. Redwood provides release notes, known issues, and upgrade/rollback guidance with each patch.

Artifact integrity (Private): Redwood publishes cryptographic checksums and provenance metadata for runtime artifacts used in Private deployments.

Security: Redwood monitors vulnerability disclosures and will provide security updates for supported LTS lines, including out-of-band releases for critical issues when appropriate. Redwood will communicate remediation guidance via security advisories and release notes.
—

If you want, I can also send a short “FAQ-style” version that’s easier to circulate internally.

Best,
Avery

—
Avery Johnson
Enterprise Account Executive
Redwood Inference
avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com

title: Re: Private deployment security review  audit log export to customer bucket

To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: IT Security Review <security-review@northstarbank.com>

Subject: Private deployment security review  audit log export to customer bucket

Hi Ava,

For our Private deployment we need audit logs exported to our AWS account (S3). Our requirements:
- customer-owned bucket
- encryption at rest (KMS)
- no long-lived access keys
- predictable object paths for retention + ingestion

Can Redwood support this? If yes, please share the auth model and a sample bucket policy.

Thanks,
Morgan

---
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Morgan Lee <morgan.lee@northstarbank.com>
Cc: IT Security Review <security-review@northstarbank.com>, Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Feb 9, 2025 at 9:05 AM
Subject: Re: Private deployment security review  audit log export to customer bucket

Hi Morgan,

Yes  we are implementing audit log export from Redwood Private to customer-owned object storage. For AWS we will use AssumeRole (short-lived) with an ExternalId, and we do not require long-lived access keys.

Looping in Diego (Private Deployments Eng) to confirm details + timeline.

Best,
Ava

---
From: Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Morgan Lee <morgan.lee@northstarbank.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, IT Security Review <security-review@northstarbank.com>
Date: Sun, Feb 9, 2025 at 11:22 AM
Subject: Re: Private deployment security review  audit log export to customer bucket

Hi Morgan,

Confirming: the exporter writes JSONL (optionally gzipped) into an S3 prefix you control. We can support SSE-KMS with a customer key ARN.

Auth: IRSA in the Private EKS cluster > AssumeRole into a customer-provided role ARN. We will require ExternalId (fail closed if missing).

Attaching:
- a short overview PDF
- an example bucket policy to scope `s3:PutObject` to a prefix

Were targeting a design-partner rollout this month; Ava can coordinate enablement once your Private cluster is ready.

Diego Fernandez
Staff Engineer, Private Deployments
Redwood Inference

[Attachment: example-s3-bucket-policy.json]

From: IT Security Review <security-review@northstarbank.com>
To: Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Morgan Lee <morgan.lee@northstarbank.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 10, 2025 at 8:17 AM
Subject: Re: Private deployment security review  audit log export to customer bucket

Thanks. Two questions:
1) Do you ever need `s3:ListBucket`? Our policy forbids it except for specific services.
2) Do audit logs include request bodies/prompts?

---
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: IT Security Review <security-review@northstarbank.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwoodinference.com>, Morgan Lee <morgan.lee@northstarbank.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 10, 2025 at 9:41 AM
Subject: Re: Private deployment security review  audit log export to customer bucket

Jumping in from Security.

1) We are designing the exporter to avoid `ListBucket`. The minimal set is `PutObject` (and `AbortMultipartUpload` if multipart is used).
2) Audit logs are administrative/control-plane events (actor/action/resource/result + request_id). We do not include prompt or completion content in audit events.

Well also share our formal requirements page as soon as its published internally.

Aisha Rahman
Security & Compliance
Redwood Inference

title: RBAC v2: exec update + migration risk review
preview:
From: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwoodinference.com>, Daniel Carter <daniel.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 6, 2025 at 8:12 AM PT
Subject: RBAC v2: exec update + migration risk review

Team,

I need an exec-level update on RBAC v2 today for the Q1 enterprise readiness review.

Please reply-all with:
1) Current timeline (discovery/design/implementation/rollout) and what is at risk.
2) Top migration risks (Hosted + Dedicated + Private), including likelihood/impact.
3) Compliance implications: what we can credibly claim for SOC 2 / enterprise questionnaires once v2 ships, and what remains “in progress.”
4) Concrete actions (owner + date) to reduce risk, especially deny-by-default rollout + break-glass controls + auditability.

Keep it crisp (bullet points are fine). If there’s something you need from me (resourcing, prioritization, sequencing), ask directly.

Aisha
Dr. Aisha Rahman
CISO, Redwood Inference


---

From: Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwoodinference.com>, Daniel Carter <daniel.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 6, 2025 at 9:03 AM PT
Subject: Re: RBAC v2: exec update + migration risk review

Aisha + team — status + timeline as of this morning.

Timeline (current plan)
- Discovery & inventory (in progress; week 2/3)
  - Output: canonical permission inventory + endpoint coverage report + naming conventions
  - Tracking: Linear ENG-18241 (inventory) + shared coverage spreadsheet
- Design (starts next week; 3–5 weeks)
  - Output: RBAC v2 design overview + ADRs + audit log schema updates
  - Tracking: PM-642 (PRD + success metrics)
- Implementation (6–10 weeks, overlaps with design)
  - Shared authz library + gateway middleware + endpoint annotations + policy bundle + Console role mgmt MVP
- Rollout & validation (4–8 weeks)
  - Dogfood + pilot accounts (1 Private, 1 Dedicated) behind flags, then staged rollout

Key milestones (targets)
- Permission catalog v1 freeze for implementation: Feb 21
- RBAC v2 design sign-off: Mar 14
- First end-to-end (gateway -> control plane -> audit logging) in staging: Apr 11
- Pilot enablement start: late April / early May (dependent on inventory coverage)

What’s at risk
- Inventory completeness / coverage gates: we’re finding endpoints without consistent checks; needs quick remediation ownership.
- Console role management MVP scope: we need to keep MVP tight (templates + bindings + permission viewer/search) to avoid slipping.
- Private installer bootstrap details: scoping + initial admin bindings need alignment with customer IdP constraints.

Requested decisions from execs (Aisha/Priya)
- Confirm we will ship with deny-by-default on all annotated endpoints, with a staged flag plan (dual-read) to avoid outages.
- Confirm break-glass workflow requirement (time-bound elevation + alerting + post-use review) is non-negotiable for enterprise launch.

I can circulate a 1-pager with the above + owners by EOD.

Talia
Program Manager


---

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwoodinference.com>, Daniel Carter <daniel.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 6, 2025 at 10:18 AM PT
Subject: Re: RBAC v2: exec update + migration risk review

Adding migration risks + mitigations (security eng view). Top 6:

1) “Silent allow” gaps (missing annotations or bypass routes)
- Likelihood: Medium (based on early endpoint audit)
- Impact: High (least-privilege claims undermined)
- Mitigation:
  - Coverage gate in CI: fail if security-relevant endpoints are unannotated (owner: Ethan/Benji, target: Mar 7)
  - Regression tests for critical actions (owner: Leila, target: Mar 21)
  - Explicit hot-path review for streaming routes (we already saw one weak spot in prior work)

2) Deny spikes during migration (customer-facing 403s)
- Likelihood: Medium
- Impact: High (outage-like symptoms + support load)
- Mitigation:
  - Dual-read mode + metrics comparison (RBAC v1 vs v2 decisions) (owner: Kaitlyn/Jordan, target: Mar 28)
  - “Why denied” admin guidance + denial reason codes (owner: Felix/Chloe + Naomi for copy, target: Apr 4)

3) Stale authz decisions due to caching
- Likelihood: Medium
- Impact: Medium/High (privilege persistence after role change)
- Mitigation:
  - Short TTL + scoped cache keys + invalidate-on-binding-change hooks (owner: Jordan, target: Apr 4)
  - Metrics: cache hit rate + decision age + deny/allow drift (owner: Logan, target: Apr 11)

4) Scope model confusion (org vs project vs env vs cluster)
- Likelihood: High (design still in flux)
- Impact: Medium (overprivileged defaults or blocking workflows)
- Mitigation:
  - Keep v2 scope primitives limited (org + project + environment; cluster only for Private admin) (owner: Naomi + Ines, target: Mar 14)
  - Publish a single scope matrix for top workflows (routing policy edits, audit export, key mgmt) (owner: Naomi/Talia, target: Mar 14)

5) Break-glass misuse / lack of monitoring
- Likelihood: Low/Medium
- Impact: High
- Mitigation:
  - Time-bound elevation, approvals, alerting to SecOps, and mandatory post-use review (owner: Fiona + Marcus, target: Mar 21)

6) Audit log “authz decision” fields incomplete/inconsistent across services
- Likelihood: Medium
- Impact: High for enterprise evidence + investigations
- Mitigation:
  - Schema update + exporter mapping (owner: Kevin, target: Apr 11)
  - Require decision context on deny + allow for sensitive actions (owner: Allison, target: Apr 18)

If helpful, I can turn the above into a risk register format with likelihood/impact scoring + contingency plans.

Marcus
Security Engineering Lead


---

From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwoodinference.com>, Daniel Carter <daniel.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 6, 2025 at 11:37 AM PT
Subject: Re: RBAC v2: exec update + migration risk review

Compliance/evidence implications (what we can say vs what’s still “in progress”):

What RBAC v2 enables us to credibly claim (once rolled out to Hosted + Dedicated + Private control plane)
- Least privilege: fine-grained permissions tied to concrete product actions (key mgmt, role mgmt, routing policy edits, audit export, retention settings, private deployment admin ops).
- Deny-by-default enforcement: consistent middleware/interceptor behavior; no implicit “admin can do everything” shortcuts beyond explicit break-glass.
- Separation of duties: built-in role templates (e.g., Security Admin vs Deployment Operator vs Billing Admin vs Read-only Auditor) with explicit permission matrices.
- Auditability of access decisions: audit log entries include subject, action, scope, decision allow/deny, denial reason code, and policy version.
- Evidence artifacts: versioned permission catalog + policy bundle release artifacts + CI coverage reports + integration tests demonstrating enforcement.

What will remain “in progress” / not claimed for v2
- Customer-defined ABAC/policy scripting (out of scope)
- Perfectly granular data-plane action scoping for all runtime inference requests (we’re focusing on control plane + admin actions first)
- Long-tail legacy endpoints not classified as security-relevant until inventory completes (we’ll be explicit about phased coverage)

SOC 2 / questionnaire packaging (Olga/Daniel, please sanity check)
- We should have a standard evidence packet: design overview + ADRs, permission inventory, role template matrix, audit log schema example (allow + deny), break-glass SOP, test coverage summary.
- We should pre-write answers for “how do you ensure authorization is enforced?” and “how do you detect/handle unauthorized access attempts?” including monitoring/alerts.

Open question for Aisha/Priya
- Do we want to require audit log export permissions be separate from “read audit logs” (to prevent bulk export by default)? I recommend yes for regulated accounts.

Naomi
PM, Security & Compliance


---

From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwoodinference.com>, Daniel Carter <daniel.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 6, 2025 at 1:09 PM PT
Subject: Re: RBAC v2: exec update + migration risk review

Agree with the plan and the non-negotiables (deny-by-default + break-glass controls + auditable decisions).

Resourcing / sequencing commitments from Platform:
- Gateway enforcement + control plane endpoint annotations are prioritized over new feature work through March.
- We will staff a “coverage strike team” for the endpoint audit remediation (Benji + Brian + service owners); goal is to eliminate high-risk unannotated admin endpoints before pilots.
- We’ll treat Private bootstrap/admin binding flows as a first-class deliverable (not an afterthought). Vivek’s team will get weekly checkpoints.

On Naomi’s question: yes, we should split “read audit logs” from “export audit logs” and have export default to Security Admin + Auditor only (not Deployment Operator).

If we run into a timeline slip, I’d rather cut Console role editor polish than compromise on enforcement coverage + logging.

Priya
VP Engineering, Platform


---

From: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>

6) Evidence packet outline for SOC 2 / enterprise questionnaires (what we’ll ship + what’s phased)

  - Dual-read mode + metrics comparison (RBAC v1 vs v2 decisions) (owner: Kaitlyn/Jordan, target: Mar 28)
  - “Why denied” admin guidance + denial reason codes (owner: Felix/Chloe + Naomi for copy, target: Apr 4)

3) Stale authz decisions due to caching
- Likelihood: Medium
- Impact: Medium/High (privilege persistence after role change)
- Mitigation:
  - Short TTL + scoped cache keys + invalidate-on-binding-change hooks (owner: Jordan, target: Apr 4)
  - Metrics: cache hit rate + decision age + deny/allow drift (owner: Logan, target: Apr 11)

4) Scope model confusion (org vs project vs env vs cluster)
- Likelihood: High (design still in flux)
- Impact: Medium (overprivileged defaults or blocking workflows)
- Mitigation:
  - Keep v2 scope primitives limited (org + project + environment; cluster only for Private admin) (owner: Naomi + Ines, target: Mar 14)
  - Publish a single scope matrix for top workflows (routing policy edits, audit export, key mgmt) (owner: Naomi/Talia, target: Mar 14)

5) Break-glass misuse / lack of monitoring
- Likelihood: Low/Medium
- Impact: High
- Mitigation:
  - Time-bound elevation, approvals, alerting to SecOps, and mandatory post-use review (owner: Fiona + Marcus, target: Mar 21)

6) Audit log “authz decision” fields incomplete/inconsistent across services
- Likelihood: Medium
- Impact: High for enterprise evidence + investigations
- Mitigation:
  - Schema update + exporter mapping (owner: Kevin, target: Apr 11)
  - Require decision context on deny + allow for sensitive actions (owner: Allison, target: Apr 18)

If helpful, I can turn the above into a risk register format with likelihood/impact scoring + contingency plans.

Marcus
Security Engineering Lead


---

From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwoodinference.com>, Daniel Carter <daniel.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 6, 2025 at 11:37 AM PT
Subject: Re: RBAC v2: exec update + migration risk review

Compliance/evidence implications (what we can say vs what’s still “in progress”):

What RBAC v2 enables us to credibly claim (once rolled out to Hosted + Dedicated + Private control plane)
- Least privilege: fine-grained permissions tied to concrete product actions (key mgmt, role mgmt, routing policy edits, audit export, retention settings, private deployment admin ops).
- Deny-by-default enforcement: consistent middleware/interceptor behavior; no implicit “admin can do everything” shortcuts beyond explicit break-glass.
- Separation of duties: built-in role templates (e.g., Security Admin vs Deployment Operator vs Billing Admin vs Read-only Auditor) with explicit permission matrices.
- Auditability of access decisions: audit log entries include subject, action, scope, decision allow/deny, denial reason code, and policy version.
- Evidence artifacts: versioned permission catalog + policy bundle release artifacts + CI coverage reports + integration tests demonstrating enforcement.

What will remain “in progress” / not claimed for v2
- Customer-defined ABAC/policy scripting (out of scope)
- Perfectly granular data-plane action scoping for all runtime inference requests (we’re focusing on control plane + admin actions first)
- Long-tail legacy endpoints not classified as security-relevant until inventory completes (we’ll be explicit about phased coverage)

SOC 2 / questionnaire packaging (Olga/Daniel, please sanity check)
- We should have a standard evidence packet: design overview + ADRs, permission inventory, role template matrix, audit log schema example (allow + deny), break-glass SOP, test coverage summary.
- We should pre-write answers for “how do you ensure authorization is enforced?” and “how do you detect/handle unauthorized access attempts?” including monitoring/alerts.

Open question for Aisha/Priya
- Do we want to require audit log export permissions be separate from “read audit logs” (to prevent bulk export by default)? I recommend yes for regulated accounts.

Naomi
PM, Security & Compliance


---

From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwoodinference.com>, Daniel Carter <daniel.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Feb 6, 2025 at 1:09 PM PT
Subject: Re: RBAC v2: exec update + migration risk review

Agree with the plan and the non-negotiables (deny-by-default + break-glass controls + auditable decisions).

Resourcing / sequencing commitments from Platform:
- Gateway enforcement + control plane endpoint annotations are prioritized over new feature work through March.
- We will staff a “coverage strike team” for the endpoint audit remediation (Benji + Brian + service owners); goal is to eliminate high-risk unannotated admin endpoints before pilots.
- We’ll treat Private bootstrap/admin binding flows as a first-class deliverable (not an afterthought). Vivek’s team will get weekly checkpoints.

On Naomi’s question: yes, we should split “read audit logs” from “export audit logs” and have export default to Security Admin + Auditor only (not Deployment Operator).

If we run into a timeline slip, I’d rather cut Console role editor polish than compromise on enforcement coverage + logging.

Priya
VP Engineering, Platform
